documentation:
![quimb logo](https://github.com/jcmgray/quimb/blob/HEAD/docs/_static/logo-banner.png?raw=true)

[![Tests](https://github.com/jcmgray/quimb/actions/workflows/tests.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/jcmgray/quimb/actions/workflows/tests.yml)
[![Code Coverage](https://codecov.io/gh/jcmgray/quimb/branch/main/graph/badge.svg)](https://codecov.io/gh/jcmgray/quimb)
[![Code Quality](https://app.codacy.com/project/badge/Grade/3c7462a3c45f41fd9d8f0a746a65c37c)](https://www.codacy.com/gh/jcmgray/quimb/dashboard?utm_source=github.com&utm_medium=referral&utm_content=jcmgray/quimb&utm_campaign=Badge_Grade)
[![Documentation Status](https://readthedocs.org/projects/quimb/badge/?version=latest)](http://quimb.readthedocs.io/en/latest/?badge=latest)
[![JOSS Paper](http://joss.theoj.org/papers/10.21105/joss.00819/status.svg)](https://doi.org/10.21105/joss.00819)
[![PyPI](https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/quimb?color=teal)](https://pypi.org/project/quimb/)
[![Anaconda-Server Badge](https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/quimb/badges/version.svg)](https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/quimb)

[`quimb`](https://github.com/jcmgray/quimb) is an easy but fast python library for *'quantum information many-body'* calculations, focusing primarily on **tensor networks**. The code is hosted on [github](https://github.com/jcmgray/quimb), and docs are hosted on [readthedocs](http://quimb.readthedocs.io/en/latest/). Functionality is split in two:

---

The `quimb.tensor` module contains tools for working with **tensors and tensor networks**. It has a particular focus on automatically handling arbitrary geometry, e.g. beyond 1D and 2D lattices. With this you can:

- construct and manipulate arbitrary (hyper) graphs of tensor networks
- automatically [contract](https://cotengra.readthedocs.io), optimize and draw networks
- use various backend array libraries such as [jax](https://jax.readthedocs.io) and [torch](https://pytorch.org/) via [autoray](https://github.com/jcmgray/autoray/)
- run specific MPS, PEPS, MERA and quantum circuit algorithms, such as DMRG & TEBD

![tensor pic](https://github.com/jcmgray/quimb/blob/HEAD/docs/_static/rand-tensor.svg?raw=true)

---

The core `quimb` module contains tools for reference **'exact'** quantum calculations, where the states and operator are represented as either `numpy.ndarray` or `scipy.sparse` **matrices**. With this you can:

- construct operators in complicated tensor spaces
- find groundstates, excited states and do time evolutions, including with [slepc](https://slepc.upv.es/)
- compute various quantities including entanglement measures
- take advantage of [numba](https://numba.pydata.org) accelerations
- stochastically estimate $\mathrm{Tr}f(X)$ quantities

![matrix pic](https://github.com/jcmgray/quimb/blob/HEAD/docs/_static/rand-herm-matrix.svg?raw=true)

---

The **full documentation** can be found at: [quimb.readthedocs.io](https://quimb.readthedocs.io). Contributions of any sort are very welcome - please see the [contributing guide](https://github.com/jcmgray/quimb/blob/main/.github/CONTRIBUTING.md). [Issues](https://github.com/jcmgray/quimb/issues) and [pull requests](https://github.com/jcmgray/quimb/pulls) are hosted on [github](https://github.com/jcmgray/quimb). For other questions and suggestions, please use the [discussions page](https://github.com/jcmgray/quimb/discussions).

modules.name: quimb.experimental.autojittn
documentation:
Decorator for automatically just in time compiling tensor network functions.

TODO:

```
- [ ] go via an intermediate pytree / array function, that could be shared
      e.g. with the TNOptimizer class.
```

functions.name: quimb.experimental.autojittn.autojit_tn
documentation:
Decorate a tensor network function to be just in time compiled / traced.
This traces solely array operations resulting in a completely static
computational graph with no side-effects. The resulting function can be
much faster if called repeatedly with only numeric changes, or hardware
accelerated if a library such as `jax` is used.

# Parameters

fn

: The function to be decorated. It should take as its first argument a
  {class}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_core.TensorNetwork` and return either act
  inplace on it or return a raw scalar or array.

decorator

: The decorator to use to wrap the underlying array function. For example
  `jax.jit`. Defaults to `autoray.autojit`.

check_inputs

: Whether to check the inputs to the function every call to see if a new
  compiled function needs to be generated. If `False` the same compiled
  function will be used for all inputs which might be incorrect. Defaults
  to `True`.

decorator_opts

: Options to pass to the decorator, e.g. `backend` for
  `autoray.autojit`.

classes.name: quimb.experimental.autojittn.AutojittedTN
documentation: Class to hold the `autojit_tn` decorated function callable.

modules.name: quimb.utils
documentation: Misc utility functions.

functions.name: quimb.utils.check_opt
documentation:
Check whether `value` takes one of `valid` options, and raise an
informative error if not.

functions.name: quimb.utils.find_library
documentation:
Check if library is installed.

Parameters
----------
x : str
    Name of library

Returns
-------
bool
    If library is available.

functions.name: quimb.utils.raise_cant_find_library_function
documentation:
Return function to flag up a missing necessary library.

This is simplify the task of flagging optional dependencies only at the
point at which they are needed, and not earlier.

Parameters
----------
x : str
    Name of library
extra_msg : str, optional
    Make the function print this message as well, for additional
    information.

Returns
-------
callable
    A mock function that when called, raises an import error specifying
    the required library.

functions.name: quimb.utils.deprecated
documentation: Mark a function as deprecated, and indicate the new name.

functions.name: quimb.utils.ensure_dict
documentation: Make sure `x` is a `dict`, creating an empty one if `x is None`.

functions.name: quimb.utils.pairwise
documentation: Iterate over each pair of neighbours in `iterable`.

functions.name: quimb.utils.print_multi_line
documentation: Print multiple lines, with a maximum width.

functions.name: quimb.utils.format_number_with_error
documentation:
Given `x` with error `err`, format a string showing the relevant
digits of `x` with two significant digits of the error bracketed, and
overall exponent if necessary.

# Parameters

x

: The value to print.

err

: The error on `x`.

# Returns

str

# Examples

> ```pycon
> >>> print_number_with_uncertainty(0.1542412, 0.0626653)
> '0.154(63)'
> ```
>
> ```pycon
> >>> print_number_with_uncertainty(-128124123097, 6424)
> '-1.281241231(64)e+11'
> ```

functions.name: quimb.utils.save_to_disk
documentation: Save an object to disk using joblib.dump.

functions.name: quimb.utils.load_from_disk
documentation: Load an object form disk using joblib.load.

functions.name: quimb.utils.gen_bipartitions
documentation:
Generate all unique bipartitions of `it`. Unique meaning
`(1, 2), (3, 4)` is considered the same as `(3, 4), (1, 2)`.

functions.name: quimb.utils.tree_register_container
documentation:
Register a new container type for use with `tree_map` and
`tree_apply`.

# Parameters

cls

: The container type to register.

mapper

: A function that takes `f`, `tree` and `is_leaf` and returns a new
  tree of type `cls` with `f` applied to all leaves.

applier

: A function that takes `f`, `tree` and `is_leaf` and applies `f`
  to all leaves in `tree`.

functions.name: quimb.utils.is_not_container
documentation:
The default function to determine if an object is a leaf. This simply
checks if the object is an instance of any of the registered container
types.

functions.name: quimb.utils.tree_map
documentation:
Map `f` over all leaves in `tree`, returning a new pytree.

# Parameters

f

: A function to apply to all leaves in `tree`.

tree

: A nested sequence of tuples, lists, dicts and other objects.

is_leaf

: A function to determine if an object is a leaf, `f` is only applied
  to objects for which `is_leaf(x)` returns `True`.

# Returns

pytree

functions.name: quimb.utils.tree_iter
documentation:
Iterate over all leaves in `tree`.

# Parameters

f

: A function to apply to all leaves in `tree`.

tree

: A nested sequence of tuples, lists, dicts and other objects.

is_leaf

: A function to determine if an object is a leaf, `f` is only applied
  to objects for which `is_leaf(x)` returns `True`.

functions.name: quimb.utils.tree_apply
documentation:
Apply `f` to all leaves in `tree`, no new pytree is built.

# Parameters

f

: A function to apply to all leaves in `tree`.

tree

: A nested sequence of tuples, lists, dicts and other objects.

is_leaf

: A function to determine if an object is a leaf, `f` is only applied
  to objects for which `is_leaf(x)` returns `True`.

functions.name: quimb.utils.tree_flatten
documentation:
Flatten `tree` into a list of leaves.

# Parameters

tree

: A nested sequence of tuples, lists, dicts and other objects.

is_leaf

: A function to determine if an object is a leaf, only objects for which
  `is_leaf(x)` returns `True` are returned in the flattened list.

# Returns

objs

: The flattened list of leaf objects.

(ref_tree)

: If `get_ref` is `True`, a reference tree, with leaves of type
  `Leaf`, is returned which can be used to reconstruct the original
  tree.

functions.name: quimb.utils.tree_unflatten
documentation:
Unflatten `objs` into a pytree of the same structure as `tree`.

# Parameters

objs

: A sequence of objects to be unflattened into a pytree.

tree

: A nested sequence of tuples, lists, dicts and other objects, the objs
  will be inserted into a new pytree of the same structure.

is_leaf

: A function to determine if an object is a leaf, only objects for which
  `is_leaf(x)` returns `True` will have the next item from `objs`
  inserted. By default checks for the `Leaf` object inserted by
  `tree_flatten(..., get_ref=True)`.

# Returns

pytree

functions.name: quimb.utils.default_to_neutral_style
documentation: Wrap a function or method to use the neutral style by default.

functions.name: quimb.utils.autocorrect_kwargs
documentation:
A decorator that suggests the right keyword arguments if you get them
wrong. Useful for functions with many specific options.

# Parameters

func

: The function to decorate.

valid_kwargs

: The valid keyword arguments for `func`, if not given these are
  inferred from the function signature.

classes.name: quimb.utils.continuous_progbar

methods.name: quimb.utils.continuous_progbar.cupdate
documentation:
'Continuous' update of progress bar.

# Parameters

x

: Current position within the range `[self.start, self.stop]`.

A continuous version of tqdm, so that it can be updated with a float
within some pre-given range, rather than a number of steps.

# Parameters

args

: Stopping point (and starting point if `len(args) == 2`) of window
  within which to evaluate progress.

total

: The number of steps to represent the continuous progress with.

kwargs

: Supplied to `tqdm.tqdm`

classes.name: quimb.utils.Verbosify
documentation:
Decorator for making functions print their inputs. Simply for
illustrating a MPI example in the docs.

classes.name: quimb.utils.oset

methods.name: quimb.utils.oset.from_dict
documentation: Public method makes sure to copy incoming dictionary.

An ordered set which stores elements as the keys of dict (ordered as of
python 3.6). 'A few times' slower than using a set directly for small
sizes, but makes everything deterministic.

classes.name: quimb.utils.LRU
documentation:
Least recently used dict, which evicts old items. Taken from python
collections OrderedDict docs.

modules.name: quimb.calc
documentation:
Functions for more advanced calculations of quantities and properties of
quantum objects.

functions.name: quimb.calc.fidelity
documentation:
Fidelity between two quantum states. By default, the unsquared fidelity
is used, equivalent in the pure state case to `|<psi|phi>|`.

# Parameters

p1

: First state.

p2

: Second state.

squared

: Whether to use the squared convention or not, by default False.

# Returns

float

functions.name: quimb.calc.purify
documentation:
Take state rho and purify it into a wavefunction of squared
dimension.

Parameters
----------
rho : operator
    Density operator to purify.

Returns
-------
vector :
    The purified ket.

functions.name: quimb.calc.kraus_op
documentation:
Operate on a mixed state with a set of kraus operators:

$$
\sigma = \sum_k E_k \rho E_k^{\dagger}
$$

# Parameters

rho

: The density operator to perform operation on.

Ek

: The Kraus operator(s).

dims

: The subdimensions of `rho`.

where

: Which of the subsystems to apply the operation on.

check

: Where to check `sum_k(Ek.H @ Ek) == 1`.

# Returns

sigma

: The state after the kraus operation.

# Examples

The depolarising channel:

```python3
In [1]: import quimb as qu

In [2]: rho = qu.rand_rho(2)

In [3]: I, X, Y, Z = (qu.pauli(s) for s in 'IXYZ')

In [4]: [qu.expec(rho, A) for A in (X, Y, Z)]
Out[4]: [-0.652176185230622, -0.1301762132792484, 0.22362918368272583]

In [5]: p = 0.1

In [6]: Ek = [
   ...:     (1 - p)**0.5 * I,
   ...:     (p / 3)**0.5 * X,
   ...:     (p / 3)**0.5 * Y,
   ...:     (p / 3)**0.5 * Z
   ...: ]

In [7]: sigma = qu.kraus_op(rho, Ek)

In [8]: [qu.expec(sigma, A) for A in (X, Y, Z)]
Out[8]: [-0.5652193605332058, -0.11281938484201527, 0.1938119591916957]
```

functions.name: quimb.calc.projector
documentation:
Compute the projector for the target `eigenvalue` of operator
`A`.

# Parameters

A

: The hermitian observable. If a tuple is supplied, assume this is the
  eigendecomposition of the observable.

eigenvalue

: The target eigenvalue to construct the projector for, default: 1.

tol

: The tolerance with which to select all eigenvalues.

autoblock

: Whether to use automatic symmetry exploitation.

# Returns

P

: The projector onto the target eigenspace.

functions.name: quimb.calc.measure
documentation:
Measure state `p` with observable `A`, collapsing the state in the
process and returning the relevant eigenvalue.

$$
\left| \psi \right\rangle \rightarrow
\frac{
    P_i \left| \psi \right\rangle
}{
    \sqrt{\left\langle \psi \right| P_i \left| \psi \right\rangle}
}
$$

and

$$
\rho \rightarrow
\frac{
    P_i \rho P_i^{\dagger}
}{
    \text{Tr} \left[ P_i \rho \right]
}
$$

along with the corresponding eigenvalue $\lambda_i$.

# Parameters

p

: The quantum state to measure.

A

: The hermitian operator corresponding to an observable. You can supply
  also a pre-diagonalized operator here as a tuple of eigenvalues and
  eigenvectors.

tol

: The tolerance within which to group eigenspaces.

eigenvalue

: If specified, deterministically collapse to this result. Otherwise
  randomly choose a result as in 'real-life'.

# Returns

result

: The result of the measurement.

p_after

: The quantum state post measurement.

functions.name: quimb.calc.simulate_counts
documentation:
Simulate measuring each qubit of `p` in the computational basis,
producing output like that of `qiskit`.

# Parameters

p

: The quantum state, assumed to be normalized, as either a ket or density
  operator.

C

: The number of counts to perform.

phys_dim

: The assumed size of the subsystems of `p`, defaults to 2 for qubits.

# Returns

results

: The counts for each bit string measured.

# Examples

Simulate measuring the state of each qubit in a GHZ-state:

```python3
>>> import quimb as qu
>>> psi = qu.ghz_state(3)
>>> qu.simulate_counts(psi, 1024)
{'000': 514, '111': 510}
```

functions.name: quimb.calc.dephase
documentation:
Dephase `rho` by amount `p`, that is, mix it
with the maximally mixed state:

> rho -> (1 - p) * rho + p * I / d

# Parameters

rho

: The state.

p

: The final proportion of identity.

rand_rank

: If given, dephase with a random diagonal operator with this many
  non-zero entries. If float, proportion of full size.

# Returns

rho_dephase

: The dephased density operator.

functions.name: quimb.calc.entropy
documentation:
Compute the (von Neumann) entropy.

Parameters
----------
a : operator or 1d array
    Positive operator or list of positive eigenvalues.
rank : int (optional)
    If operator has known rank, then a partial decomposition can be
    used to accelerate the calculation.

Returns
-------
float
    The von Neumann entropy.

See Also
--------
mutinf, entropy_subsys, entropy_subsys_approx

functions.name: quimb.calc.mutinf
documentation:
Find the mutual information for a bipartition of a state.

That is, `H(A) + H(B) - H(AB)`, for von Neumann entropy `H`, and two
subsystems A and B.

# Parameters

p

: State, can be vector or operator.

dims

: Internal dimensions of state.

sysa

: Index of first subsystem, A.

sysb

: Index of second subsystem, B.

rank

: If known, the rank of rho_ab, to speed calculation of `H(AB)` up.
  For example, if `p` comes from tracing out three qubits from a
  system, then its rank is 2^3 = 8 etc.

# Returns

float

# See Also

entropy, mutinf_subsys, entropy_subsys_approx

functions.name: quimb.calc.check_dims_and_indices
documentation:
Make sure all indices found in the tuples `syss` are in
`range(len(dims))`.

functions.name: quimb.calc.mutinf_subsys
documentation:
Calculate the mutual information of two subsystems of a pure state,
possibly using an approximate lanczos method for large subsytems.

# Parameters

psi_abc

: Tri-partite pure state.

dims

: The sub dimensions of the state.

sysa

: The index(es) of the subsystem(s) to consider part of 'A'.

sysb

: The index(es) of the subsystem(s) to consider part of 'B'.

approx_thresh

: The size of subsystem at which to switch to the approximate lanczos
  method. Set to `None` to never use the approximation.

approx_opts

: Supplied to {func}`entropy_subsys_approx`, if used.

# Returns

float

: The mutual information.

# See Also

mutinf, entropy_subsys, entropy_subsys_approx, logneg_subsys

functions.name: quimb.calc.schmidt_gap
documentation:
Find the schmidt gap of the bipartition of `psi_ab`. That is, the
difference between the two largest eigenvalues of the reduced density
operator.

# Parameters

psi_ab

: Bipartite state.

dims

: The sub-dimensions of the state.

sysa

: The indices of which dimensions to calculate the entropy for.

# Returns

float

functions.name: quimb.calc.tr_sqrt
documentation: Return the trace of the sqrt of a positive semidefinite operator.

functions.name: quimb.calc.partial_transpose
documentation:
Partial transpose of a density operator.

Parameters
----------
p : operator or vector
    The state to partially transpose.
dims : tuple(int), optional
    The internal dimensions of the state.
sysa : sequence of int
    The indices of 'system A', everything else assumed to be 'system B'.

Returns
-------
operator

See Also
--------
logneg, negativity

functions.name: quimb.calc.partial_transpose_norm
documentation:
Compute the norm of the partial transpose for (log)-negativity,
taking a shortcut (trace sqrt of reduced subsytem), when system is a
vector.

functions.name: quimb.calc.logneg
documentation:
Compute logarithmic negativity between two subsytems.
This is defined as log_2( | rho\_\{AB}^\{T_B} | ). This only handles
bipartitions (including pure states efficiently), and will not trace
anything out.

# Parameters

p

: State to compute logarithmic negativity for.

dims

: The internal dimensions of `p`.

sysa

: Index of the first subsystem, A, relative to `dims`.

# Returns

float

# See Also

negativity, partial_transpose, logneg_subsys_approx

functions.name: quimb.calc.logneg_subsys
documentation:
Compute the logarithmic negativity between two subsystems of a pure
state, possibly using an approximate lanczos for large subsystems. Uses
a special method if the two subsystems form a bipartition of the state.

# Parameters

psi_abc

: Tri-partite pure state.

dims

: The sub dimensions of the state.

sysa

: The index(es) of the subsystem(s) to consider part of 'A'.

sysb

: The index(es) of the subsystem(s) to consider part of 'B'.

approx_thresh

: The size of subsystem at which to switch to the approximate lanczos
  method. Set to `None` to never use the approximation.

approx_opts

: Supplied to {func}`~quimb.logneg_subsys_approx`, if used.

# Returns

float

: The logarithmic negativity.

# See Also

logneg, mutinf_subsys, logneg_subsys_approx

functions.name: quimb.calc.negativity
documentation:
Compute negativity between two subsytems.

This is defined as (| rho\_\{AB}^\{T_B} | - 1) / 2. If `len(dims) > 2`,
then the non-target dimensions will be traced out first.

# Parameters

p

: State to compute logarithmic negativity for.

dims

: The internal dimensions of `p`.

sysa

: Index of the first subsystem, A, relative to `dims`.

# Returns

float

# See Also

logneg, partial_transpose, negativity_subsys_approx

functions.name: quimb.calc.concurrence
documentation:
Concurrence of two-qubit state.

If `len(dims) > 2`, then the non-target dimensions will be traced out
first.

# Parameters

p

: State to compute concurrence for.

dims

: The internal dimensions of `p`.

sysa

: Index of the first subsystem, A, relative to `dims`.

sysb

: Index of the first subsystem, B, relative to `dims`.

# Returns

float

functions.name: quimb.calc.one_way_classical_information
documentation:
One way classical information for two qubit density operator.

Parameters
----------
p_ab : operator
    State of two qubits
prjs : sequence of matrices
   The POVMs.
precomp_func : bool, optional
    Whether to return a pre-computed function, closed over the actual
    state.

Returns
-------
float or callable
    The one-way classical information or the function to compute it for
    the given state which takes a set of POVMs as its single argument.

functions.name: quimb.calc.quantum_discord
documentation:
Quantum Discord for two qubit density operator.

If `len(dims) > 2`, then the non-target dimensions will be traced out
first.

# Parameters

p

: State to compute quantum discord for.

dims

: The internal dimensions of `p`.

sysa

: Index of the first subsystem, A, relative to `dims`.

sysb

: Index of the first subsystem, B, relative to `dims`.

# Returns

float

functions.name: quimb.calc.trace_distance
documentation:
Trace distance between two states:

$$
\delta(\rho, \sigma)
=
\frac{1}{2} \left| \rho - \sigma \right|_\mathrm{tr}
$$

If two wavefunctions are supplied the trace distance will be computed via
the more efficient expression:

$$
\delta(|\psi\rangle\langle\psi|, |\phi\rangle\langle\phi|)
=
\sqrt{1 - \langle \psi | \phi \rangle^2}
$$

# Parameters

p1

: The first state.

p2

: The second state.

# Returns

float

functions.name: quimb.calc.cprint
documentation:
Print a state in the computational basis.

Parameters
----------
psi : vector
    The pure state.
prec : int, optional
    How many significant figures to print.

Examples
--------

    >>> cprint(rand_ket(2**2))
    (-0.0751069-0.192635j) |00>
      (0.156837-0.562213j) |01>
    (-0.307381+0.0291168j) |10>
      (-0.14302+0.707661j) |11>

    >>> cprint(w_state(4))
    (0.5+0j) |0001>
    (0.5+0j) |0010>
    (0.5+0j) |0100>
    (0.5+0j) |1000>

functions.name: quimb.calc.decomp
documentation:
Decomposes an operator via the Hilbert-schmidt inner product.

Can both print the decomposition or return it.

# Parameters

a

: Operator to decompose.

fn

: Function to generate operator/state to decompose with.

fn_args :

: Sequence of args whose permutations will be supplied to `fn`.

fn_d

: The dimension of the operators that `fn` produces.

nmlz_func

: Function to produce a normlization coefficient given the `n`
  permutations of operators that will be produced.

mode :

: String, include `'p'` to print the decomp and/or `'c'` to
  return OrderedDict, sorted by size of contribution.

tol :

: Print operators with contirbution above `tol` only.

# Returns

None or OrderedDict:

: Pauli operator name and expec with `a`.

# See Also

pauli_decomp, bell_decomp

functions.name: quimb.calc.correlation
documentation:
Calculate the correlation between two sites given two operators.

# Parameters

p

: State to compute correlations for, ignored if `precomp_func=True`.

A

: Operator to act on first subsystem.

B

: Operator to act on second subsystem.

sysa

: Index of first subsystem.

sysb

: Index of second subsystem.

dims

: Internal dimensions of `p`, will be assumed to be qubits if not
  given.

sparse

: Whether to compute with sparse operators.

precomp_func

: Whether to return result or single arg function closed
  over precomputed operator.

# Returns

float or callable

: The correlation, \<ab> - \<a>\<b>, or a function to compute for an
  arbitrary state.

functions.name: quimb.calc.pauli_correlations
documentation:
Calculate the correlation between sites for a list of operator pairs
choisen from the pauli matrices.

# Parameters

p

: State to compute correlations for. Ignored if `precomp_func=True`.

ss

: List of pairs specifiying pauli matrices.

sysa

: Index of first site.

sysb

: Index of second site.

sum_abs

: Whether to sum over the absolute values of each correlation

precomp_func

: whether to return the values or a single argument
  function closed over precomputed operators etc.

# Returns

list of float, list of callable, float or callable

: Either the value(s) of each correlation or the function(s) to compute
  the correlations for an arbitrary state, depending on `sum_abs` and
  `precomp_func`.

functions.name: quimb.calc.ent_cross_matrix
documentation:
Calculate the pair-wise function ent_fn  between all sites or blocks
of a state.

Parameters
----------
p : ket or density operator
    State.
sz_blc : int
    Size of the blocks to partition the state into. If the number of
    individual sites is not a multiple of this then the final (smaller)
    block will be ignored.
ent_fn : callable
    Bipartite function, notionally entanglement
calc_self_ent : bool
    Whether to calculate the function for each site
    alone, purified. If the whole state is pure then this is the
    entanglement with the whole remaining system.
upscale : bool, optional
    Whether, if sz_blc != 1, to upscale the results so that the output
    array is the same size as if it was.

Returns
-------
2D-array
    array of pairwise ent_fn results.

functions.name: quimb.calc.is_degenerate
documentation:
Check if operator has any degenerate eigenvalues, determined relative
to mean spacing of all eigenvalues.

Parameters
----------
op : operator or 1d-array
    Operator or assumed eigenvalues to check degeneracy for.
tol : float
    How much closer than evenly spaced the eigenvalue gap has to be
    to count as degenerate.

Returns
-------
n_dgen : int
    Number of degenerate eigenvalues.

functions.name: quimb.calc.is_eigenvector
documentation:
Determines whether a vector is an eigenvector of an operator.

# Parameters

x

: Vector to check.

A

: Matrix to check.

tol

: The variance must be smaller than this value.

# Returns

bool

: Whether `A @ x = l * x` for some scalar `l`.

functions.name: quimb.calc.page_entropy
documentation:
Calculate the page entropy, i.e. expected entropy for a subsytem
of a random state in Hilbert space.

Parameters
----------
sz_subsys : int
    Dimension of subsystem.
sz_total : int
    Dimension of total system.

Returns
-------
s : float
    Entropy in bits.

functions.name: quimb.calc.heisenberg_energy
documentation:
Get the analytic isotropic heisenberg chain ground energy for length L.
Useful for testing. Assumes the heisenberg model is defined with spin
operators not pauli matrices (overall factor of 2 smaller). Taken from [1].

[1] Nickel, Bernie. "Scaling corrections to the ground state energy
of the spin-½ isotropic anti-ferromagnetic Heisenberg chain." Journal of
Physics Communications 1.5 (2017): 055021

Parameters
----------
L : int
    The length of the chain.

Returns
-------
energy : float
    The ground state energy.

variables.name: quimb.calc.entropy_subsys
documentation:
Calculate the entropy of a pure states' subsystem, optionally switching
to an approximate lanczos method when the subsystem is very large.

# Parameters

psi_ab

: Bipartite state.

dims

: The sub-dimensions of the state.

sysa

: The indices of which dimensions to calculate the entropy for.

approx_thresh

: The size of sysa at which to switch to the approx method. Set to
  `None` to never use the approximation.

approx_opts

: Supplied to {func}`entropy_subsys_approx`, if used.

# Returns

float

: The subsytem entropy.

# See Also

entropy, entropy_subsys_approx, mutinf_subsys

variables.name: quimb.calc.tr_sqrt_subsys
documentation:
Compute the trace sqrt of a subsystem, possibly using an approximate
lanczos method when the subsytem is big.

# Parameters

psi_ab

: Bipartite state.

dims

: The sub-dimensions of the state.

sysa

: The indices of which dimensions to calculate the trace sqrt for.

approx_thresh

: The size of sysa at which to switch to the approx method. Set to
  `None` to never use the approximation.

approx_opts

: Supplied to {func}`tr_sqrt_subsys_approx`, if used.

# Returns

float

: The subsytem entropy.

# See Also

tr_sqrt, tr_sqrt_subsys_approx, partial_transpose_norm

variables.name: quimb.calc.pauli_decomp
documentation: Decompose an operator into Paulis.

variables.name: quimb.calc.bell_decomp
documentation: Decompose an operator into bell-states.

modules.name: quimb.experimental.cluster_update
documentation: Implementation of arbitrary geometry wavefunction cluster update.

functions.name: quimb.experimental.cluster_update.gate_inds_nn_fit
documentation:
Gate two nearest neighbor outer indices, using full fitting of
reduced tensors with respect to the environment. This is more accurate
than a simple reduced gate when restricting the bond dimension.

# Parameters

G

: The gate to fit.

ind1, ind2

: The indices to gate.

max_bond

: The maximum bond dimension to use. If `None`, use the maximum
  bond dimension that the tensors currently share.

method

: The method to use for fitting.

pregauge

: How many times to locally canonize from the purified environment
  tensor to both the left and right reduced tensors.

init_simple_guess

: Whether to use a 'simple update' guess for the initial guess. This
  can be quite high quality already if pregauging is used.

steps

: The number of steps to use for fitting, can be `0` in which case
  the initial guess is used, which in conjuction with the
  envinronment pregauging can still be quite high quality.

inplace

: Whether to update the tensor network in place.

contract_opts

: Supplied to
  {meth}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_core.TensorNetwork.contract`.

classes.name: quimb.experimental.cluster_update.ClusterUpdateNNGen
documentation:
Cluster update for arbitrary geometry nearest neighbor hamiltonians.
This keeps track of simple update style gauges, in order to approximately
partial trace beyond `cluster_radius` and form an approximate environment
for two nearest neighbor sites that be used to fit the gate with higher
quality than simple update only.
TensorNetwork.gate_inds_nn_fit =gate_inds_nn_fit
TensorNetwork.gate_inds_nn_fit_ = gate_inds_nn_fit

modules.name: quimb.experimental.tn_marginals
documentation:
Helper functions for computing marginals of classical partition functions /
SAT problems.

modules.name: quimb.schematic
documentation: Draw psuedo-3D diagrams using matplotlib.

functions.name: quimb.schematic.parse_style_preset
documentation:
Parse a one or more style presets plus manual kwargs.

Parameters
----------
presets : dict
    The dictionary of presets.
preset : str or sequence of str
    The name of the preset(s) to use. If multiple, later presets take
    precedence.
kwargs
    Any additional manual keyword arguments are added to the style and
    override the presets.

Returns
-------
style : dict

functions.name: quimb.schematic.axonometric_project
documentation:
Project the 3D location `(i, j, k)` onto the 2D plane, using
the axonometric projection with the given angles `a` and `b`.

The `xscale`, `yscale` and `zscale` parameters can be used to
scale the axes, including flipping them.

# Parameters

i, j, k

: The 3D coordinates of the point to project.

a, b

: The left and right angles to displace x and y axes, from horizontal,
  in degrees.

xscale, yscale, zscale

: The scaling factor for the x, y and z axes. If negative, the axis
  is flipped.

# Returns

x, y

: The 2D coordinates of the projected point.

functions.name: quimb.schematic.coo_to_zorder
documentation:
Given the coordinates of a point in 3D space, return a z-order value
that can be used to draw it on top of other elements in the diagram.
Take into account the scaling of the axes, so that the z-ordering
is correct even if the axes flipped.

functions.name: quimb.schematic.get_wong_color
documentation:
Get a color by name, optionally modifying its alpha, hue, saturation
or value.

These colorblind friendly colors were ppularized in an article by Wong
(https://www.nature.com/articles/nmeth.1618) but originally come from
Okabe & Ito (https://jfly.uni-koeln.de/color/).

Parameters
----------
which : {'blue', 'orange', 'green', 'red', 'yellow', 'pink', 'bluedark'}
    The name of the color to get.
alpha : float, optional
    The alpha channel value to set for the color. Default is 1.0.
hue_factor : float, optional
    The amount to shift the hue of the color. Default is 0.0.
sat_factor : float, optional
    The amount to scale the saturation of the color. Default is 1.0.
val_factor : float, optional
    The amount to scale the value of the color. Default is 1.0.

Returns
-------
color : tuple[float, float, float, float]
    The RGBA color as a tuple of floats.

functions.name: quimb.schematic.mod_sat
documentation:
Modify the luminosity of color `c`, optionally set the `alpha`
channel, and return the final color as a RGBA tuple.

functions.name: quimb.schematic.auto_colors
documentation:
Generate a nice sequence of `nc` colors. By default this uses an
interpolation between the colorblind friendly colors of Okabe & Ito in hue
sorted order, with luminosity moderated by a sine function to increase
local distinguishability.

# Parameters

nc

: The number of colors to generate.

alpha

: The alpha channel value to set for all colors. Default is 1.0.

default_sequence

: If `True`, take from the default sequence of 7 colors, un-sorted and
  un-modulated.

# Returns

colors : list\[tuple[float, float, float, float]\]

functions.name: quimb.schematic.darken_color
documentation: Take `color` and darken it by `factor`.

functions.name: quimb.schematic.average_color
documentation: Take a sequence of colors and return the RMS average in RGB space.

functions.name: quimb.schematic.jitter_color
documentation: Take `color` and add a random offset to each of its components.

functions.name: quimb.schematic.set_coloring_seed
documentation:
Set the seed for the random color generator.

Parameters
----------
seed : int
    The seed to use.

functions.name: quimb.schematic.hash_to_nvalues
documentation: Hash the string `s` to `nval` different floats in the range [0, 1].

functions.name: quimb.schematic.hash_to_color
documentation:
Generate a random color for a string `s`.

# Parameters

s

: The string to generate a color for.

hmin

: The minimum hue value.

hmax

: The maximum hue value.

smin

: The minimum saturation value.

smax

: The maximum saturation value.

vmin

: The minimum value value.

vmax

: The maximum value value.

# Returns

color

: A tuple of floats in the range [0, 1] representing the RGB color.

functions.name: quimb.schematic.mean
documentation: Get the mean of a list of numbers.

functions.name: quimb.schematic.distance
documentation: Get the distance between two points, in arbtirary dimensions.

functions.name: quimb.schematic.get_angle
documentation: Get the angle between the line from p1 to p2 and the x-axis.

functions.name: quimb.schematic.get_rotator_and_inverse
documentation:
Get a rotation matrix that rotates points by theta radians about
the origin and then translates them by offset.

functions.name: quimb.schematic.get_control_points
documentation:
Get two points that can be used to construct a bezier curve that
passes smoothly through the angle `pa`, `pb`, `pc`.

functions.name: quimb.schematic.gen_points_around
documentation: Generate points around a circle.

classes.name: quimb.schematic.Drawing

methods.name: quimb.schematic.Drawing.text
documentation:
Place text at the specified coordinate.

# Parameters

coo

: The 2D or 3D coordinate of the text. If 3D, the coordinate will be
  projected onto the 2D plane, and a z-order will be assigned.

text

: The text to place.

preset

: A preset style to use for the text.

kwargs

: Specific style options passed to `matplotlib.axes.Axes.text`.

methods.name: quimb.schematic.Drawing.text_between
documentation:
Place text between two coordinates.

# Parameters

cooa, coob

: The 2D or 3D coordinates of the text endpoints. If 3D, the
  coordinates will be projected onto the 2D plane, and a z-order
  will be assigned based on average z-order of the endpoints.

text

: The text to place.

center

: The position of the text along the line, where 0.0 is the start and
  1.0 is the end. Default is 0.5.

preset

: A preset style to use for the text.

kwargs

: Specific style options passed to `matplotlib.axes.Axes.text`.

methods.name: quimb.schematic.Drawing.label_ax
documentation:
Place text at the specified location, using the axis coordinates
rather than 2D or 3D data coordinates.

# Parameters

x, y

: The x and y positions of the text, relative to the axis.

text

: The text to place.

preset

: A preset style to use for the text.

kwargs

: Specific style options passed to `matplotlib.axes.Axes.text`.

methods.name: quimb.schematic.Drawing.label_fig
documentation:
Place text at the specified location, using the figure coordinates
rather than 2D or 3D data coordinates.

# Parameters

x, y

: The x and y positions of the text, relative to the figure.

text

: The text to place.

preset

: A preset style to use for the text.

kwargs

: Specific style options passed to `matplotlib.axes.Axes.text`.

methods.name: quimb.schematic.Drawing.circle
documentation:
Draw a circle at the specified coordinate.

# Parameters

coo

: The 2D or 3D coordinate of the circle. If 3D, the coordinate will
  be projected onto the 2D plane, and a z-order will be assigned.

preset

: A preset style to use for the circle.

kwargs

: Specific style options passed to `matplotlib.patches.Circle`.

methods.name: quimb.schematic.Drawing.wedge
documentation:
Draw a wedge at the specified coordinate.

# Parameters

coo

: The 2D or 3D coordinate of the wedge. If 3D, the coordinate will
  be projected onto the 2D plane, and a z-order will be assigned.

theta1

: The angle in degrees of the first edge of the wedge.

theta2

: The angle in degrees of the second edge of the wedge.

preset

: A preset style to use for the wedge.

kwargs

: Specific style options passed to `matplotlib.patches.Wedge`.

methods.name: quimb.schematic.Drawing.dot
documentation:
Draw a small circle with no border. Alias for circle with defaults
`radius=0.1` and `linewidth=0.0`.

# Parameters

coo

: The 2D or 3D coordinate of the dot. If 3D, the coordinate will
  be projected onto the 2D plane, and a z-order will be assigned.

preset

: A preset style to use for the dot.

kwargs

: Specific style options passed to `matplotlib.patches.Circle`.

methods.name: quimb.schematic.Drawing.regular_polygon
documentation:
Draw a regular polygon at the specified coordinate.

# Parameters

coo

: The 2D or 3D coordinate of the polygon. If 3D, the coordinate will
  be projected onto the 2D plane, and a z-order will be assigned.

n

: The number of sides of the polygon.

orientation

: The orientation of the polygon in radians. Default is 0.0.

preset

: A preset style to use for the polygon.

kwargs

: Specific style options passed to `matplotlib.patches.Polygon`.

methods.name: quimb.schematic.Drawing.marker
documentation:
Draw a 'marker' at the specified coordinate. This is a shorthand for
creating polygons with shape specified by a single character.

# Parameters

coo

: The 2D or 3D coordinate of the marker. If 3D, the coordinate will
  be projected onto the 2D plane, and a z-order will be assigned.

marker

: The marker shape to draw. One of `"o.v^<>sDphH8"`.

preset

: A preset style to use for the marker.

kwargs

: Specific style options.

methods.name: quimb.schematic.Drawing.cube
documentation:
Draw a cube at the specified coordinate, which must be 3D.

# Parameters

coo

: The 3D coordinate of the cube. The coordinate will
  be projected onto the 2D plane, and a z-order will be assigned.

preset

: A preset style to use for the cube.

kwargs

: Specific style options passed to `matplotlib.patches.Polygon`.

methods.name: quimb.schematic.Drawing.line
documentation:
Draw a line between two coordinates.

# Parameters

cooa, coob

: The 2D or 3D coordinates of the line endpoints. If 3D, the
  coordinates will be projected onto the 2D plane, and a z-order
  will be assigned based on average z-order of the endpoints.

stretch

: Stretch the line by this factor. 1.0 is no stretch, 0.5 is half
  length, 2.0 is double length. Default is 1.0.

arrowhead

: Draw an arrowhead at the end of the line. Default is False. If a
  dict, it is passed as keyword arguments to the arrowhead method.

text_between

: Add text along the line.

preset

: A preset style to use for the line.

kwargs

: Specific style options passed to `matplotlib.lines.Line2D`.

# See Also

Drawing.arrowhead

methods.name: quimb.schematic.Drawing.line_offset
documentation:
Draw a line between two coordinates, but curving out by a given
offset perpendicular to the line.

# Parameters

cooa, coob

: The 2D or 3D coordinates of the line endpoints. If 3D, the
  coordinates will be projected onto the 2D plane, and a z-order
  will be assigned based on average z-order of the endpoints.

offset

: The offset of the curve from the line, as a fraction of the total
  line length. This is always processed in the 2D projected plane.

midlength

: The length of the middle straight section, as a fraction of the
  total line length. Default is 0.5.

arrowhead

: Draw an arrowhead at the end of the line. Default is False. If a
  dict, it is passed as keyword arguments to the arrowhead method.

text_between

: Add text along the line.

relative

: If `True` (the default), then `offset` is taken as a fraction
  of the line length, else in absolute units.

preset

: A preset style to use for the line.

kwargs

: Specific style options passed to `curve`.

methods.name: quimb.schematic.Drawing.arrowhead
documentation:
Draw just a arrowhead on the line between `cooa` and `coob`.

# Parameters

cooa, coob

: The coordinates of the start and end of the line. If 3D, the
  coordinates will be projected onto the 2D plane, and a z-order
  will be assigned based on average z-order of the endpoints.

reverse

: Reverse the direction by switching `cooa` and `coob`. If
  `"both"`, draw an arrowhead in both directions. Default is
  False.

center

: The position of the arrowhead along the line, where 0 is the start
  and 1 is the end. Default is 0.5.

width

: The width of the arrowhead. Default is 0.05.

length

: The length of the arrowhead. Default is 0.1.

preset

: A preset style to use for the arrowhead, including the above
  options.

kwargs

: Specific style options passed to `matplotlib.lines.Line2D`.

methods.name: quimb.schematic.Drawing.curve
documentation:
Draw a smooth line through the given coordinates.

# Parameters

coos

: The 2D or 3D coordinates of the line. If 3D, the coordinates will
  be projected onto the 2D plane, and a z-order will be assigned
  based on average z-order of the endpoints.

smoothing

: The amount of smoothing to apply to the curve. 0.0 is no smoothing,
  1.0 is maximum smoothing. Default is 0.5.

preset

: A preset style to use for the curve.

kwargs

: Specific style options passed to `matplotlib.lines.Line2D`.

methods.name: quimb.schematic.Drawing.shape
documentation:
Draw a closed shape with (sharp) corners at the given coordinates.

# Parameters

coos

: The coordinates of the corners' of the shape.

preset

: A preset style to use for the shape.

kwargs

: Specific style options passed to `matplotlib.patches.PathPatch`.

# See Also

Drawing.patch

methods.name: quimb.schematic.Drawing.patch
documentation:
Draw a closed smooth patch through given coordinates.

# Parameters

coos

: The coordinates of the 'corners' of the patch, the outline is
  guaranteed to pass through these points.

smoothing

: The smoothing factor, the higher the smoother. The default is
  0.5.

preset

: A preset style to use for the patch.

kwargs

: Specific style options passed to `matplotlib.patches.PathPatch`.

# See Also

Drawing.shape, Drawing.curve

methods.name: quimb.schematic.Drawing.patch_around
documentation:
Draw a patch around the given coordinates, by contructing a convex
hull around the points, optionally including an extra uniform or per
coordinate radius.

# Parameters

coos

: The coordinates of the points to draw the patch around. If 3D, the
  coordinates will be projected onto the 2D plane, and a z-order will
  be assigned based on *min* z-order of the endpoints.

radius

: The radius of the patch around each point. If a sequence, must be
  the same length as `coos`. Default is 0.0.

resolution

: The number of points to use pad around each point. Default is 12.

preset

: A preset style to use for the patch.

kwargs

: Specific style options passed to `matplotlib.patches.PathPatch`.

methods.name: quimb.schematic.Drawing.patch_around_circles
documentation:
Draw a smooth patch around two circles.

# Parameters

cooa

: The coordinates of the center of the first circle. If 3D, the
  coordinates will be projected onto the 2D plane, and a z-order
  will be assigned based on average z-order of the endpoints.

ra

: The radius of the first circle.

coob

: The coordinates of the center of the second circle. If 3D, the
  coordinates will be projected onto the 2D plane, and a z-order
  will be assigned based on average z-order of the endpoints.

rb

: The radius of the second circle.

padding

: The amount of padding to add around the circles. Default is 0.2.

pinch

: If or how much to pinch the patch in between the circles.
  Default is to match the padding.

preset

: A preset style to use for the patch.

kwargs

: Specific style options passed to `matplotlib.patches.PathPatch`.

# See Also

Drawing.patch

Draw 2D or pseudo-3D diagrams using matplotlib. This handles the
axonometric projection and the z-ordering of the elements, as well as named
preset styles for repeated elements, and the automatic adjustment of the
figure limits. It also has basic support for drawing smooth curves and
shaded areas around certain elements automatically.

# Parameters

background

: The background color of the figure, defaults to transparent.

drawcolor

: The default color to draw lines and text in.

shapecolor

: The default color to fill shapes with.

a

: The axonometric angle of the x-axis in degrees.

b

: The axonometric angle of the y-axis in degrees.

xscale

: A factor to scale the x-axis by.

yscale

: A factor to scale the y-axis by.

zscale

: A factor to scale the z-axis by.

presets

: A dictionary of named style presets. When you add an element to the
  drawing, you can specify a preset name to use as default styling.

ax

: The axes to draw on. If None, a new figure is created.

kwargs

: Passed to `plt.figure` if `ax` is None.

modules.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder
documentation:
Tools for defining and constructing sparse operators with:

> - arbitrary geometries,
> - numba acceleration,
> - support for reduced bases,
> - efficient parallelization,

and optionally producing:

> - sparse matrix form
> - matrix product operators,
> - dict of local gates form
> - VMC 'coupled configs' form

Currently only supports composing operators which are sums of products of
diagonal or anti-diagonal real dimension 2 operators.

TODO:

```
- [ ] fix sparse matrix being built in opposite direction
- [ ] product of operators generator (e.g. for PEPS DMRG)
- [ ] complex and single precision support (lower priority)
- [ ] support for non-diagonal and qudit operators (lower priority)
```

DONE:

```
- [x] use compact bitbasis
- [x] design interface for HilbertSpace / OperatorBuilder interaction
- [x] automatic symbolic jordan wigner transformation
- [x] numba accelerated coupled config
- [x] general definition and automatic 'symbolic' jordan wigner
- [x] multithreaded sparse matrix construction
- [x] LocalHam generator (e.g. for simple update, normal PEPS algs)
- [x] automatic MPO generator
```

functions.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.get_local_size
documentation:
Given global size n, and a rank in [0, world_size), return the size of
the portion assigned to this rank.

functions.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.get_local_range
documentation:
Given global size n, and a rank in [0, world_size), return the range of
indices assigned to this rank.

functions.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.parse_edges_to_unique
documentation:
Given a list of edges, return a sorted list of unique sites and edges.

Parameters
----------
edges : Iterable[tuple[hashable, hashable]]]
    The edges to parse.

Returns
-------
sites : list of hashable
    The unique sites in the edges, sorted.
edges : list of (hashable, hashable)
    The unique edges, sorted.

functions.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.simplify_single_site_ops
documentation:
Simplify a sequence of operators acting on the same site.

Parameters
----------
coeff : float or complex
    The coefficient of the operator sequence.
ops : tuple of str
    The operator sequence.

Returns
-------
new_coeff : float or complex
    The simplified coefficient.
new_op : str
    The single, simplified operator that the sequence maps to, up to
    scalar multiplication.

Examples
--------

    >>> simplify_single_site_ops(1.0, ('+', 'z', 'z', 'z', 'z', '-'))
    (1.0, 'n')

    >>> simplify_single_site_ops(1.0, ("x", "y", "z"))
    (-1j, 'I')

functions.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.get_nth_bit
documentation:
Get the nth bit of val.

Examples
--------

    >>> get_nth_bit(0b101, 1)
    0

functions.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.flip_nth_bit
documentation:
Flip the nth bit of val.

Examples
--------

    >>> bin(flip_nth_bit(0b101, 1))
    0b111

functions.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.comb
documentation: Compute the binomial coefficient n choose k.

functions.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.get_all_equal_weight_bits
documentation: Get an array of all 'bits' (integers), with n bits, and k of them set.

functions.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.bit_to_rank
documentation:
Given a bitstring b, return the rank of the bitstring in the
basis of all bitstrings of length n with k bits set. Adapted from
https://dlbeer.co.nz/articles/kwbs.html.

functions.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.rank_to_bit
documentation:
Given a rank r, return the bitstring of length n with k bits set
that has rank r in the basis of all bitstrings of length n with k
bits set. Adapted from https://dlbeer.co.nz/articles/kwbs.html.

functions.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.get_all_equal_weight_flatconfigs
documentation: Get every flat configuration of length n with k bits set.

functions.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.flatconfig_to_bit
documentation: Given a flat configuration, return the corresponding bitstring.

functions.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.flatconfig_to_rank
documentation:
Given a flat configuration `flatconfig`, return the rank of the
bitstring in the basis of all bitstrings of length `n` with `k` bits
set. Adapted from <https://dlbeer.co.nz/articles/kwbs.html>.

functions.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.rank_to_flatconfig
documentation:
Given a rank `r`, return the flat configuration of length `n`
with `k` bits set that has rank `r` in the basis of all bitstrings of
length `n` with `k` bits set. Adapted from
<https://dlbeer.co.nz/articles/kwbs.html>.

functions.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.product_of_bits
documentation:
Get the outer product of two bit arrays.

Parameters
----------
b1, b2 : array
    The bit arrays to take the outer product of.
n2 : int
    The number of bits in ``b2``

functions.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.get_number_bitbasis
documentation:
Create a bit basis with number conservation.

# Parameters

nk_pairs

: Each element is a pair (n, k) where n is the number of bits, and k is
  the number of bits that are set. The basis will be the product of all
  supplied pairs.

# Returns

basis

: An array of integers, each representing a bit string. The size will be
  `prod(comb(n, k) for n, k in nk_pairs)`.

# Examples

A single number conserving basis:

> ```pycon
> >>> for i, b in enumerate(get_number_bitbasis((4, 2))):
> >>>    print(f"{i}: {b:0>4b}")
> 0: 0011
> 1: 0101
> 2: 0110
> 3: 1001
> 4: 1010
> 5: 1100
> ```

A product of two number conserving bases, e.g. n_up and n_down:

> ```pycon
> >>> for b in get_number_bitbasis((3, 2), (3, 1)):
> >>>     print(f"{b:0>6b}")
> 011001
> 011010
> 011100
> 101001
> 101010
> 101100
> 110001
> 110010
> 110100
> ```

functions.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.build_bitmap
documentation: Build of map of bits to linear indices, suitable for use with numba.

functions.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.build_coupling_numba
documentation:
Create a sparse nested dictionary of how each term couples each
local site configuration to which other local site configuration, and
with what coefficient, suitable for use with numba.

# Parameters

term_store

: The terms of the operator.

site_to_reg

: A function that maps a site to a linear register index.

# Returns

coupling_map

: A nested numba dictionary of the form
  `{term: {reg: {bit_in: (bit_out, coeff), ...}, ...}, ...}`.

functions.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.coupled_flatconfigs_numba
documentation:
Get the coupled flat configurations for a given flat configuration
and coupling map.

# Parameters

flatconfig

: The flat configuration to get the coupled configurations for.

coupling_map

: A nested numba dictionary of the form
  `{term: {reg: {bit_in: (bit_out, coeff), ...}, ...}, ...}`.

# Returns

coupled_flatconfigs

: A list of coupled flat configurations, each with the corresponding
  coefficient.

coeffs

: The coefficients for each coupled flat configuration.

functions.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.build_coo_numba
documentation:
Build an operator in COO form, using the basis `bits` and the

: `coupling_map`, optionally multithreaded.

# Parameters

bits

: An array of integers, each representing a bit string.

coupling_map

: A nested numba dictionary of couplings. The outermost key is the term
  index, the next key is the register, and the innermost key is the bit
  index. The value is a tuple of (coupled bit index, coupling
  coefficient).

parallel

: Whether to parallelize the computation. If an integer is given, it
  specifies the number of threads to use.

# Returns

data

: The non-zero elements of the operator.

cis

: The row indices of the non-zero elements.

cjs

: The column indices of the non-zero elements.

functions.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.heisenberg_from_edges
documentation:
Create a Heisenberg Hamiltonian on the graph defined by `edges`.

# Parameters

edges

: The edges, as pairs of hashable 'sites', that define the graph.
  Multiple edges are allowed, and will be treated as a single edge.

j

: The Heisenberg exchange coupling constant(s). If a single float is
  given, it is used for all three terms. If a tuple of three floats is
  given, they are used for the xx, yy, and zz terms respectively. Note
  that positive values of `j` correspond to antiferromagnetic coupling.

b

: The magnetic field strength(s). If a single float is given, it is used
  taken as a z-field. If a tuple of three floats is given, they are used
  for the x, y, and z fields respectively.

hilbert_space

: The Hilbert space to use. If not given, one will be constructed
  automatically from the edges.

# Returns

H : SparseOperatorBuilder

methods.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.HilbertSpace.rand_config
documentation:
Get a random configuration, optionally requiring it has `k` bits

methods.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.HilbertSpace.sites
documentation: The ordered tuple of all sites in the Hilbert space.

methods.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.HilbertSpace.nsites
documentation: The total number of sites in the Hilbert space.

methods.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.HilbertSpace.get_size
documentation:
Compute the size of this Hilbert space, optionally taking into
account number / z-spin conservation.

# Parameters

k

: If provided, compute the size of number conserving subspace(s):

  ```
  - If a single int, compute the size of the subspace with
    ``k`` particles / up states: ``comb(nsites, k)``.
  - If a tuple of (int, int), compute the size of the subspace
    of the product of spaces where each pair (n, k) corresponds
    to n sites with k particles / up states. The sum of every n
    should equal ``nsites``.
  ```

methods.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.HilbertSpace.get_bitbasis
documentation:
Get a basis for the Hilbert space, in terms of an integer bitarray,
optionally taking into account number / z-spin conservation.

# Parameters

k

: If provided, get the basis for a number conserving subspace(s):

  ```
  - If a single int, compute the size of the subspace with
    ``k`` particles / up states: ``comb(nsites, k)``.
  - If a tuple of (int, int), compute the size of the subspace
    of the product of spaces where each pair (n, k) corresponds
    to n sites with k particles / up states. The sum of every n
    should equal ``nsites``.
  ```

dtype

: The dtype of the bitarray, should be an integer type with at least
  `nsites` bits.

# Returns

bits

: The basis, each integer element being a binary representation of a
  configuration.

Take a set of 'sites' (any sequence of sortable, hashable objects), and
map this into a 'register' or linearly indexed range, optionally using a
particular ordering. One can then calculate the size of the Hilbert space,
including number conserving subspaces, and get a compact 'bitbasis' with
which to construct sparse operators.

# Parameters

sites

: The sites to map into a linear register. If an integer, simply use
  `range(sites)`.

order

: If provided, use this to order the sites. If a callable, it should be a
  sorting key. If a sequence, it should be a permutation of the sites,
  and `key=order.index` will be used.

The Hilbert space of the operator. Created from the sites seen

methods.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.SparseOperatorBuilder.site_to_reg
documentation: Get the register / linear index of coordinate `site`.

methods.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.SparseOperatorBuilder.add_term
documentation:
Add a term to the operator.

# Parameters

coeff

: The overall coefficient of the term.

ops

: The operators of the term, together with the sites they act on.
  Each term should be a pair of `(operator, site)`, where
  `operator` can be:

  > - `'x'`, `'y'`, `'z'`: Pauli matrices
  > - `'sx'`, `'sy'`, `'sz'`: spin operators (i.e. scaled
  >   Pauli matrices)
  > - `'+'`, `'-'`: creation/annihilation operators
  > - `'n'`, `'sn'`, or `'h'`: number, symmetric
  >   number (n - 1/2) and hole (1 - n) operators

  And `site` is a hashable object that represents the site that
  the operator acts on.

methods.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.SparseOperatorBuilder.jordan_wigner_transform
documentation:
Transform the terms in this operator by pre-prending pauli Z
strings to all creation and annihilation operators, and then
simplifying the resulting terms.

methods.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.SparseOperatorBuilder.build_coo_data
documentation:
Build the raw data for a sparse matrix in COO format. Optionally
in a reduced k basis and in parallel.

# Parameters

k

: If provided, get the basis for a number conserving subspace(s):

  ```
  - If a single int, compute the size of the subspace with
    ``k`` particles / up states: ``comb(nsites, k)``.
  - If a tuple of (int, int), compute the size of the subspace
    of the product of spaces where each pair (n, k) corresponds
    to n sites with k particles / up states. The sum of every n
    should equal ``nsites``.
  ```

parallel

: Whether to build the matrix in parallel (multi-threaded).

# Returns

coo

: The data entries for the sparse matrix in COO format.

cis

: The row indices for the sparse matrix in COO format.

cjs

: The column indices for the sparse matrix in COO format.

N

: The total number of basis states.

methods.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.SparseOperatorBuilder.build_sparse_matrix
documentation:
Build a sparse matrix in the given format. Optionally in a reduced
k basis and in parallel.

# Parameters

k

: If provided, get the basis for a number conserving subspace(s):

  ```
  - If a single int, compute the size of the subspace with
    ``k`` particles / up states: ``comb(nsites, k)``.
  - If a tuple of (int, int), compute the size of the subspace
    of the product of spaces where each pair (n, k) corresponds
    to n sites with k particles / up states. The sum of every n
    should equal ``nsites``.
  ```

parallel

: Whether to build the matrix in parallel (multi-threaded).

# Returns

scipy.sparse matrix

methods.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.SparseOperatorBuilder.build_dense
documentation:
Get the dense (`numpy.ndarray`) matrix representation of this
operator.

methods.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.SparseOperatorBuilder.build_local_terms
documentation:
Get a dictionary of local terms, where each key is a sorted tuple
of sites, and each value is the local matrix representation of the
operator on those sites. For use with e.g. tensor network algorithms.

Note terms acting on the same sites are summed together and the size of
each local matrix is exponential in the locality of that term.

Returns
-------
Hk : dict[tuple[hashable], numpy.ndarray]
    The local terms.

methods.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.SparseOperatorBuilder.config_coupling
documentation:
Get a list of other configurations coupled to `config` by this

coupled_configs

methods.name: quimb.experimental.operatorbuilder.operatorbuilder.SparseOperatorBuilder.show
documentation: Print an ascii representation of the terms in this operator.

Object for building operators with sparse structure. Specifically,
a sum of terms, where each term is a product of operators, where each of
these local operators acts on a single site and has at most one entry per
row.

# Parameters

terms

: The terms to initialize the builder with. `add_term` is simply called
  on each of these.

hilbert_space

: The Hilbert space to build the operator in. If this is not supplied
  then a minimal Hilbert space will be constructed from the sites used,
  when required.

Tools for constructing MERA for arbitrary geometry.

functions.name: quimb.experimental.merabuilder.calc_1d_unis_isos
documentation:
Given `sites`, assumed to be in a 1D order, though not neccessarily
contiguous, calculate unitary and isometry groupings:

```
       │         │ <- new grouped site
┐   ┌─────┐   ┌─────┐   ┌
│   │ ISO │   │ ISO │   │
┘   └─────┘   └─────┘   └
│   │..│..│   │..│..│   │
┌───┐  │  ┌───┐  │  ┌───┐
│UNI│  │  │UNI│  │  │UNI│
└───┘  │  └───┘  │  └───┘
│   │ ... │   │ ... │   │
    ^^^^^^^ <- isometry groupings of size, block_size
^^^^^     ^^^^^ <- unitary groupings of size 2
```

# Parameters

sites

: The sites to apply a layer to.

block_size

: How many sites to group together per isometry block. Note that
  currently the unitaries will only ever act on blocks of size 2 across
  isometry block boundaries.

cyclic

: Whether to apply disentangler / unitaries across the boundary. The
  isometries will never be applied across the boundary, but since they
  always form a tree such a bipartition is natural.

group_from_right

: Wether to group the sites starting from the left or right. This only
  matters if `block_size` does not divide the number of sites.
  Alternating between left and right more evenly tiles the unitaries and
  isometries, especially at lower layers.

# Returns

unis

: The unitary groupings.

isos

: The isometry groupings.

functions.name: quimb.experimental.merabuilder.TTN_randtree_rand
documentation:
Return a randomly constructed tree tensor network.

# Parameters

sites

: The sites of the tensor network.

D

: The maximum bond dimension.

phys_dim

: The dimension of the physical indices.

group_size

: How many sites to group together in each tensor.

iso

: Whether to build the tree with an isometric flow towards the top.

seed

: A random seed.

kwargs

: Supplied to `TensorNetworkGenIso.empty`.

# Returns

ttn

: The tree tensor network.

classes.name: quimb.experimental.merabuilder.TensorNetworkGenIso

methods.name: quimb.experimental.merabuilder.TensorNetworkGenIso.layer_gate_raw
documentation:
Build out this MERA by placing either a new unitary, isometry or
tree tensor, given by `G`, at the sites given by `where`. This
handles propagating the lightcone of tags and marking the correct
indices of the `IsoTensor` as `left_inds`.

# Parameters

G

: The raw array to place at the sites. Its shape determines whether
  it is a unitary or isometry/tree. It should have `k + len(where)`
  dimensions. For a unitary `k == len(where)`. If it is an
  isometry/tree, `k` will generally be `1`, or `0` to 'cap' the
  MERA. The rightmost indices are those attached to the current open
  layer indices.

where

: The sites to layer the tensor above.

iso

: Whether to declare the tensor as an unitary/isometry by marking
  the left indices. If `iso = False` (a 'tree' tensor) then one
  should have `k <= 1`. Once you have such a 'tree' tensor you
  cannot place isometries or unitaries above it. It will also have
  the lightcone tags of every site. Technically one could place
  'PEPS' style tensor with `iso = False` and `k > 1` but some
  methods might break.

new_sites

: Which sites to make new open sites. If not given, defaults to the
  first `k` sites in `where`.

tags

: Custom tags to add to the new tensor, in addition to the
  automatically generated site tags.

all_site_tags

: For performance, supply all site tags to avoid recomputing them.

methods.name: quimb.experimental.merabuilder.TensorNetworkGenIso.layer_gate_fill_fn
documentation:
Build out this MERA by placing either a new unitary, isometry or
tree tensor at sites `where`, generating the data array using
`fill_fn` and maximum bond dimension `max_bond`.

# Parameters

fill_fn

: A function with signature `fill_fn(shape) -> array_like`.

operation

: The type of tensor to place.

where

: The sites to layer the tensor above.

max_bond

: The maximum bond dimension of the tensor. This only applies for
  isometries and trees and when the product of the lower dimensions
  is greater than `max_bond`.

new_sites

: Which sites to make new open sites. If not given, defaults to the
  first `k` sites in `where`.

tags

: Custom tags to add to the new tensor, in addition to the
  automatically generated site tags.

all_site_tags

: For performance, supply all site tags to avoid recomputing them.

# See Also

layer_gate_raw

methods.name: quimb.experimental.merabuilder.TensorNetworkGenIso.partial_trace
documentation:
Partial trace out all sites except those in `keep`, making use of
the lightcone structure of the MERA.

# Parameters

keep

: The sites to keep.

optimize

: The contraction ordering strategy to use.

rehearse

: Whether to rehearse the contraction rather than actually performing
  it. If:

  - `False`: perform the contraction and return the reduced density
    matrix,
  - "tn": just the lightcone tensor network is returned,
  - "tree": just the contraction tree that will be used is returned.

contract_opts

: Additional options to pass to
  {func}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_core.tensor_contract`.

# Returns

array_like

: The reduced density matrix on sites `keep`.

methods.name: quimb.experimental.merabuilder.TensorNetworkGenIso.local_expectation
documentation:
Compute the expectation value of a local operator `G` at sites
`where`. This is done by contracting the lightcone tensor network
to form the reduced density matrix, before taking the trace with
`G`.

# Parameters

G

: The local operator to compute the expectation value of.

where

: The sites to compute the expectation value at.

optimize

: The contraction ordering strategy to use.

rehearse

: Whether to rehearse the contraction rather than actually performing
  it. See {meth}`~quimb.tensor.mera.MERA.partial_trace` for details.

contract_opts

: Additional options to pass to
  {func}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_core.tensor_contract`.

# Returns

float

: The expectation value of `G` at sites `where`.

# See Also

partial_trace

methods.name: quimb.experimental.merabuilder.TensorNetworkGenIso.compute_local_expectation
documentation:
Compute the expectation value of a collection of local operators
`terms` at sites `where`. This is done by contracting the lightcone
tensor network to form the reduced density matrices, before taking the
trace with each `G` in `terms`.

# Parameters

terms

: The local operators to compute the expectation value of, keyed by
  the sites they act on.

optimize

: The contraction ordering strategy to use.

return_all

: Whether to return all the expectation values, or just the sum.

rehearse

: Whether to rehearse the contraction rather than actually performing
  it. See {meth}`~quimb.tensor.mera.MERA.partial_trace` for details.

executor

: The executor to use for parallelism.

progbar

: Whether to show a progress bar.

contract_opts

: Additional options to pass to
  {func}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_core.tensor_contract`.

methods.name: quimb.experimental.merabuilder.TensorNetworkGenIso.expand_bond_dimension
documentation:
Expand the maxmimum bond dimension of this isometric tensor network
to `new_bond_dim`. Unlike
{meth}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_core.TensorNetwork.expand_bond_dimension`
this proceeds from the physical indices upwards, and only increases a
bonds size if `new_bond_dim` is larger than product of the lower
indices dimensions.

# Parameters

new_bond_dim

: The new maximum bond dimension to expand to.

rand_strength

: The strength of random noise to add to the new array entries,
  if any.

inds_to_expand

: The indices to expand, if not all.

inplace

: Whether to expand this tensor network in place, or return a new
  one.

# Returns

TensorNetworkGenIso

A class for building generic 'isometric' or MERA like tensor network
states with arbitrary geometry. After supplying the underyling `sites` of
the problem - which can be an arbitrary sequence of hashable objects - one
places either unitaries, isometries or tree tensors layered above groups of
sites. The isometric and tree tensors effectively coarse grain blocks into
a single new site, and the unitaries generally 'disentangle' between
blocks.

classes.name: quimb.experimental.merabuilder.MERA

methods.name: quimb.experimental.merabuilder.MERA.from_fill_fn
documentation:
Create a 1D MERA using `fill_fn(shape) -> array_like` to fill the
tensors.

# Parameters

fill_fn

: A function which takes a shape and returns an array_like of that
  shape. You can override this specfically for the unitaries,
  isometries and cap tensors using the kwargs `uni_fill_fn`,
  `iso_fill_fn` and `cap_fill_fn`.

L

: The number of sites.

D

: The maximum bond dimension.

phys_dim

: The dimension of the physical indices.

block_size

: The size of the isometry blocks. Binary MERA is the default,
  ternary MERA is `block_size=3`.

cyclic

: Whether to apply disentangler / unitaries across the boundary. The
  isometries will never be applied across the boundary, but since
  they always form a tree such a bipartition is natural.

uni_fill_fn

: A function which takes a shape and returns an array_like of that
  shape. This is used to fill the unitary tensors. If `None` then
  `fill_fn` is used.

iso_fill_fn

: A function which takes a shape and returns an array_like of that
  shape. This is used to fill the isometry tensors. If `None` then
  `fill_fn` is used.

cap_fill_fn

: A function which takes a shape and returns an array_like of that
  shape. This is used to fill the cap tensors. If `None` then
  `fill_fn` is used.

kwargs

: Supplied to `TensorNetworkGenIso.__init__`.

methods.name: quimb.experimental.merabuilder.MERA.rand
documentation:
Return a random (optionally isometrized) MERA.

# Parameters

L

: The number of sites.

D

: The maximum bond dimension.

seed

: A random seed.

block_size

: The size of the isometry blocks. Binary MERA is the default,
  ternary MERA is `block_size=3`.

phys_dim

: The dimension of the physical indices.

cyclic

: Whether to apply disentangler / unitaries across the boundary. The
  isometries will never be applied across the boundary, but since
  they always form a tree such a bipartition is natural.

isometrize_method

: If given, the method to use to isometrize the MERA. If `None`
  then the MERA is not isometrized.

Replacement class for `MERA` which uses the new infrastructure and
thus has methods like `compute_local_expectation`.

modules.name: quimb.experimental.belief_propagation
documentation:
Belief propagation (BP) routines. There are three potential categorizations
of BP and each combination of them is potentially valid specific algorithm.

# 1-norm vs 2-norm BP

- 1-norm (normal): BP runs directly on the tensor network, messages have size
  `d` where `d` is the size of the bond(s) connecting two tensors or
  regions.
- 2-norm (quantum): BP runs on the squared tensor network, messages have size
  `d^2` where `d` is the size of the bond(s) connecting two tensors or
  regions. Each local tensor or region is partially traced (over dangling
  indices) with its conjugate to create a single node.

# Graph vs Hypergraph BP

- Graph (simple): the tensor network lives on a graph, where indices either
  appear on two tensors (a bond), or appear on a single tensor (are outputs).
  In this case, messages are exchanged directly between tensors.
- Hypergraph: the tensor network lives on a hypergraph, where indices can
  appear on any number of tensors. In this case, the update procedure is two
  parts, first all 'tensor' messages are computed, these are then used in the
  second step to compute all the 'index' messages, which are then fed back into
  the 'tensor' message update and so forth. For 2-norm BP one likely needs to
  specify which indices are outputs and should be traced over.

The hypergraph case of course includes the graph case, but since the 'index'
message update is simply the identity, it is convenient to have a separate
simpler implementation, where the standard TN bond vs physical index
definitions hold.

# Dense vs Vectorized vs Lazy BP

- Dense: each node is a single tensor, or pair of tensors for 2-norm BP. If all
  multibonds have been fused, then each message is a vector (1-norm case) or
  matrix (2-norm case).
- Vectorized: the same as the above, but all matching tensor update and message
  updates are stacked and performed simultaneously. This can be enormously more
  efficient for large numbers of small tensors.
- Lazy: each node is potentially a tensor network itself with arbitrary inner
  structure and number of bonds connecting to other nodes. The message are
  generally tensors and each update is a lazy contraction, which is potentially
  much cheaper / requires less memory than forming the 'dense' node for large
  tensors.

(There is also the MPS flavor where each node has a 1D structure and the
messages are matrix product states, with updates involving compression.)

Overall that gives 12 possible BP flavors, some implemented here:

- [x] (HD1BP) hyper, dense, 1-norm - this is the standard BP algorithm
- [x] (HD2BP) hyper, dense, 2-norm
- [x] (HV1BP) hyper, vectorized, 1-norm
- [ ] (HV2BP) hyper, vectorized, 2-norm
- [ ] (HL1BP) hyper, lazy, 1-norm
- [ ] (HL2BP) hyper, lazy, 2-norm
- [x] (D1BP) simple, dense, 1-norm - simple BP for simple tensor networks
- [x] (D2BP) simple, dense, 2-norm - this is the standard PEPS BP algorithm
- [ ] (V1BP) simple, vectorized, 1-norm
- [ ] (V2BP) simple, vectorized, 2-norm
- [x] (L1BP) simple, lazy, 1-norm
- [x] (L2BP) simple, lazy, 2-norm

The 2-norm methods can be used to compress bonds or estimate the 2-norm.
The 1-norm methods can be used to estimate the 1-norm, i.e. contracted value.
Both methods can be used to compute index marginals and thus perform sampling.

The vectorized methods can be extremely fast for large numbers of small
tensors, but do currently require all dimensions to match.

The dense and lazy methods can can converge messages *locally*, i.e. only
update messages adjacent to messages which have changed.

documentation:
Estimate the contraction of `tn` with hyper, vectorized, 1-norm
belief propagation, via the exponential of the Bethe free entropy.

# Parameters

tn

: The tensor network to run BP on, can have hyper indices.

messages

: Initial messages to use, if not given then uniform messages are used.

max_iterations

: The maximum number of iterations to perform.

tol

: The convergence tolerance for messages.

smudge_factor

: A small number to add to the denominator of messages to avoid division
  by zero. Note when this happens the numerator will also be zero.

damping

: The damping factor to use, 0.0 means no damping.

strip_exponent

: Whether to strip the exponent from the final result. If `True`
  then the returned result is `(mantissa, exponent)`.

info

: If specified, update this dictionary with information about the
  belief propagation run.

progbar

: Whether to show a progress bar.

# Returns

scalar or (scalar, float)
