functions.name: quimb.gen.operators.destroy

functions.name: quimb.gen.operators.ham_hubbard_hardcore

kwargs

H

: The hamiltonian.

modules.name: quimb.tensor.array_ops
documentation: Backend agnostic array operations.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.array_ops.asarray
documentation:
Maybe convert data for a tensor to use. If `array` already has a
`.shape` attribute, i.e. looks like an array, it is left as-is. Else the
elements are inspected to see which libraries' array constructor should be
used, defaulting to `numpy` if everything is builtin or numpy numbers.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.array_ops.isblocksparse
documentation: Check if `x` is a block-sparse array. Cached on class for speed.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.array_ops.isfermionic
documentation: Check if `x` is a fermionic array. Cached on class for speed.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.array_ops.fuse
documentation:
Fuse the give group or groups of axes. The new fused axes will be
inserted at the minimum index of any fused axis (even if it is not in
the first group). For example, `fuse(x, [5, 3], [7, 2, 6])` will
produce an array with axes like:

```
groups inserted at axis 2, removed beyond that.
        ......<--
(0, 1, g0, g1, 4, 8, ...)
        |   |
        |   g1=(7, 2, 6)
        g0=(5, 3)
```

# Parameters

axes_groups

: The axes to fuse. Each group of axes will be fused into a single
  axis.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.array_ops.ndim
documentation: The number of dimensions of an array.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.array_ops.multiply_diagonal
documentation: Multiply v into x as if contracting in a diagonal matrix.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.array_ops.align_axes
documentation:
Prepare a set of arrays that should be contractible along `axes`.

For example, block symmetric arrays need to have aligned sectors prior to
fusing.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.array_ops.iscomplex
documentation: Does `x` have a complex dtype?
------------- miscelleneous other backend agnostic functions -------------- #

functions.name: quimb.tensor.array_ops.norm_fro
documentation: The frobenius norm of an array.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.array_ops.sensibly_scale
documentation:
Take an array and scale it *very* roughly such that random tensor
networks consisting of such arrays do not have gigantic norms.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.array_ops._numba_find_diag_axes
documentation:
Numba-compiled array diagonal axis finder.

Parameters
----------
x : numpy.ndarray
    The array to search for diagonal axes.
atol : float
    The tolerance with which to compare to zero.

Returns
-------
diag_axes : set[tuple[int]]
    The set of pairs of axes which are diagonal.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.array_ops.find_diag_axes
documentation:
Try and find a pair of axes of `x` in which it is diagonal.

# Parameters

x

: The array to search.

atol

: Tolerance with which to compare to zero.

# Returns

tuple[int] or None

: The two axes if found else None.

# Examples

> ```pycon
> >>> x = np.array([[[1, 0], [0, 2]],
> ...               [[3, 0], [0, 4]]])
> >>> find_diag_axes(x)
> (1, 2)
> ```

Which means we can reduce `x` without loss of information to:

> ```pycon
> >>> np.einsum('abb->ab', x)
> array([[1, 2],
>        [3, 4]])
> ```

functions.name: quimb.tensor.array_ops._numba_find_antidiag_axes
documentation:
Numba-compiled array antidiagonal axis finder.

Parameters
----------
x : numpy.ndarray
    The array to search for anti-diagonal axes.
atol : float
    The tolerance with which to compare to zero.

Returns
-------
antidiag_axes : set[tuple[int]]
    The set of pairs of axes which are anti-diagonal.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.array_ops.find_antidiag_axes
documentation:
Try and find a pair of axes of `x` in which it is anti-diagonal.

# Parameters

x

: The array to search.

atol

: Tolerance with which to compare to zero.

# Returns

tuple[int] or None

: The two axes if found else None.

# Examples

> ```pycon
> >>> x = np.array([[[0, 1], [0, 2]],
> ...               [[3, 0], [4, 0]]])
> >>> find_antidiag_axes(x)
> (0, 2)
> ```

Which means we can reduce `x` without loss of information to:

> ```pycon
> >>> np.einsum('aba->ab', x[::-1, :, :])
> array([[3, 4],
>        [1, 2]])
> ```

as long as we flip the order of dimensions on other tensors corresponding
to the the same index.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.array_ops._numba_find_columns
documentation:
Numba-compiled single non-zero column axis finder.

Parameters
----------
x :  array
    The array to search.
atol : float, optional
    Absolute tolerance to compare to zero with.

Returns
-------
set[tuple[int]]
    Set of pairs (axis, index) defining lone non-zero columns.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.array_ops.find_columns
documentation:
Try and find columns of axes which are zero apart from a single index.

# Parameters

x

: The array to search.

atol

: Tolerance with which to compare to zero.

# Returns

tuple[int] or None

: If found, the first integer is which axis, and the second is which
  column of that axis, else None.

# Examples

> ```pycon
> >>> x = np.array([[[0, 1], [0, 2]],
> ...               [[0, 3], [0, 4]]])
> >>> find_columns(x)
> (2, 1)
> ```

Which means we can happily slice `x` without loss of information to:

> ```pycon
> >>> x[:, :, 1]
> array([[1, 2],
>        [3, 4]])
> ```

Chain the new function `g` on top of current function `f` like

Simple array-like object that lazily generates the actual array by

params

modules.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom_tebd

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom_tebd.edge_coloring
documentation:
Generate an edge coloring for the graph given by `edges`, using
`networkx.coloring.greedy_color`.

# Parameters

edges

: The edges of the graph.

strategy

: The strategy to use for coloring the edges. Can be:

  > - 'largest_first'
  > - 'smallest_last'
  > - 'random_sequential'
  >
  > ...

interchange

: Whether to use the interchange heuristic. Usually generates better
  colorings but can be slower.

group

: Whether to group the edges by color or return a flat list.

classes.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom_tebd.LocalHamGen

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom_tebd.LocalHamGen.nsites
documentation: The number of sites in the system.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom_tebd.LocalHamGen.items
documentation:
Iterate over all terms in the hamiltonian. This is mostly for
convenient compatibility with `compute_local_expectation`.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom_tebd.LocalHamGen.get_gate
documentation: Get the local term for pair `where`, cached.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom_tebd.LocalHamGen.get_gate_expm
documentation:
Get the local term for pair `where`, matrix exponentiated by
`x`, and cached.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom_tebd.LocalHamGen.apply_to_arrays
documentation: Apply the function `fn` to all the arrays representing terms.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom_tebd.LocalHamGen.get_auto_ordering
documentation:
Get an ordering of the terms to use with TEBD, for example. The
default is to sort the coordinates then greedily group them into
commuting sets.

# Parameters

order

: How to order the terms *before* greedily grouping them into
  commuting (non-coordinate overlapping) sets:

  > - `'sort'` will sort the coordinate pairs first.
  > - `None` will use the current order of terms which should
  >   match the order they were supplied to this `LocalHam2D`
  >   instance.
  > - `'random'` will randomly shuffle the coordinate pairs
  >   before grouping them - *not* the same as returning a
  >   completely random order.
  > - `'random-ungrouped'` will randomly shuffle the coordinate
  >   pairs but *not* group them at all with respect to
  >   commutation.

  Any other option will be passed as a strategy to
  {func}`networkx.coloring.greedy_color` to generate the ordering.

# Returns

list\[tuple[node]\]

: Sequence of coordinate pairs.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom_tebd.LocalHamGen.draw
documentation:
Plot this Hamiltonian as a network.

# Parameters

ordering

: An ordering of the termns, or an argument to be supplied to
  {meth}`quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom_tebd.LocalHamGen.get_auto_ordering`
  to generate this automatically.

show_norm

: Show the norm of each term as edge labels.

figsize

: Size of the figure, defaults to size of Hamiltonian.

fontsize

: Font size for norm labels.

legend

: Whether to show the legend of which terms are in which group.

ax

: Add to a existing set of axes.

Representation of a local hamiltonian defined on a general graph. This
combines all two site and one site terms into a single interaction per
lattice pair, and caches operations on the terms such as getting their
exponential. The sites (nodes) should be hashable and comparable.

# Parameters

H2

: The interaction terms, with each key being an tuple of nodes defining
  an edge and each value the local hamilotonian term for those two nodes.

H1

: The one site term(s). If a single array is given, assume to be the
  default onsite term for all terms. If a dict is supplied,
  the keys should represent specific coordinates like
  `(i, j)` with the values the array representing the local term for
  that site. A default term for all remaining sites can still be supplied
  with the key `None`.

# Attributes

terms

: The total effective local term for each interaction (with single site
  terms appropriately absorbed). Each key is a pair of coordinates
  `site_a, site_b` with `site_a < site_b`.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom_tebd.TEBDGen.plot
documentation:
Plot an overview of the evolution of the energy and gauge diffs.

Parameters
----------
zoom : int or 'auto', optional
    The number of iterations to zoom in on, or 'auto' to automatically
    choose a reasonable zoom level.
xscale : {'linear', 'log', 'symlog'}, optional
    The x-axis scale, for the upper plot of the entire evolution.
xscale_linthresh : float, optional
    The linear threshold for the upper symlog scale.
color_energy : str or tuple, optional
    The color to use for the energy plot.
color_gauge_diff : str or tuple, optional
    The color to use for the gauge diff plot.
hlines : dict, optional
    Add horizontal lines to the plot, with keys as labels and values
    as the y-values.
figsize : tuple, optional
    The size of the figure.

Returns
-------
fig, axs : matplotlib.Figure, tuple[matplotlib.Axes]

Generic class for performing time evolving block decimation on an
arbitrary graph, i.e. applying the exponential of a Hamiltonian using
a product formula that involves applying local exponentiated gates only.

documentation: Application of a single gate `G` at `where`.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom_tebd.SimpleUpdateGen.equilibrate
documentation:
Equilibrate the gauges with the current state (like evolving with
tau=0).

documentation: Performed after each layer of commuting gates.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom_tebd.SimpleUpdateGen.postsweep

documentation: Normalize the state and simple gauges.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom_tebd.SimpleUpdateGen.compute_energy
documentation: Default estimate of the energy.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom_tebd.SimpleUpdateGen.get_state
documentation:
Return the current state, possibly absorbing the gauges.

# Parameters

absorb_gauges

: Whether to absorb the gauges into the state or not. If `True`, a
  standard PEPS is returned with the gauges absorbed. If `` False` ``,
  the gauges are added to the tensor network but uncontracted. If
  "return", the gauges are returned separately.

# Returns

psi

: The current state.

gauges

: The current gauges, if `absorb_gauges == "return"`.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom_tebd.SimpleUpdateGen.set_state
documentation: Set the current state and possibly the gauges.

Simple update for arbitrary geometry hamiltonians.

# Parameters

psi0

: The initial state.

ham

: The local hamiltonian.

tau

: The default time step to use.

D

: The maximum bond dimension, by default the current maximum bond of
  `psi0`.

cutoff

: The singular value cutoff to use when applying gates.

imag

: Whether to evolve in imaginary time (default) or real time.

gate_opts

: Other options to supply to the gate application method,
  {meth}`quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom.TensorNetworkGenVector.gate_simple_`.

ordering

: The ordering of the terms to apply, by default this will be determined
  automatically.

second_order_reflect

: Whether to use a second order Trotter decomposition by reflecting the
  ordering.

compute_energy_every

: Compute the energy every this many steps.

compute_energy_final

: Whether to compute the energy at the end.

compute_energy_opts

: Options to supply to the energy computation method,
  {func}`quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom.TensorNetworkGenVector.compute_local_expectation_cluster`.

compute_energy_fn

: A custom function to compute the energy, with signature `fn(su)`,
  where `su` is this instance.

compute_energy_per_site

: Whether to compute the energy per site.

tol

: If not `None`, stop when either energy difference falls below this
  value, or maximum singluar value changes fall below this value.

equilibrate_every

: Equilibrate the gauges every this many steps.

equilibrate_start

: Whether to equilibrate the gauges at the start, regardless of
  `equilibrate_every`.

equilibrate_opts

: Default options to supply to the gauge equilibration method, see
  {meth}`quimb.tensor.tensor_core.TensorNetwork.gauge_all_simple`. By
  default `max_iterations` is set to 100 and `tol` to 1e-3.

callback

: A function to call after each step, with signature `fn(su)`.

keep_best

: Whether to keep track of the best state and energy.

progbar

: Whether to show a progress bar during evolution.

modules.name: quimb.tensor
documentation: Tensor and tensor network functionality.

modules.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_1d_compress
documentation:
Generic methods for compressing 1D-like tensor networks, where the tensor
network can locally have arbitrary structure and outer indices.

- [x] the direct method
- [x] the density matrix method
- [x] the zip-up method
- [x] the zip-up first method
- [x] the 1-site variational fit method, including sums of tensor networks
- [x] the 2-site variational fit method, including sums of tensor networks
- [x] the local projector method (CTMRG and HOTRG style)
- [x] the autofit method (via non-1d specific ALS or autodiff)

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_1d_compress.enforce_1d_like
documentation:
Check that `tn` is 1D-like with OBC, i.e. 1) that each tensor has
exactly one of the given `site_tags`. If not, raise a ValueError. 2) That
there are no hyper indices. And 3) that there are only bonds within sites
or between nearest neighbor sites. This issue can be optionally
automatically fixed by inserting a string of identity tensors.

# Parameters

tn

: The tensor network to check.

site_tags

: The tags to use to group and order the tensors from `tn`. If not
  given, uses `tn.site_tags`.

fix_bonds

: Whether to fix the bond structure by inserting identity tensors.

inplace

: Whether to perform the fix inplace or not.

# Raises

ValueError

: If the tensor network is not 1D-like.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_1d_compress.tensor_network_1d_compress_direct
documentation:
Compress a 1D-like tensor network using the 'direct' or 'naive' method,
that is, explicitly contracting site-wise to form a MPS-like TN,
canonicalizing in one direction, then compressing in the other. This has
the same scaling as the density matrix (dm) method, but a larger prefactor.
It can still be faster for small bond dimensions however, and is
potentially higher precision since it works in the space of singular values
directly rather than singular values squared. It is not quite optimal in
terms of error due to the compounding errors of the SVDs.

# Parameters

tn

: The tensor network to compress. Every tensor should have exactly one of
  the site tags. Each site can have multiple tensors and output indices.

max_bond

: The maximum bond dimension to compress to.

cutoff

: A dynamic threshold for discarding singular values when compressing.

site_tags

: The tags to use to group and order the tensors from `tn`. If not
  given, uses `tn.site_tags`. The tensor network built will have one
  tensor per site, in the order given by `site_tags`.

normalize

: Whether to normalize the final tensor network, making use of the fact
  that the output tensor network is in right canonical form.

canonize

: Whether to canonicalize the network in one direction before compressing
  in the other.

cutoff_mode

: The mode to use when truncating the singular values of the decomposed
  tensors. See {func}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_split`.

permute_arrays

: Whether to permute the array indices of the final tensor network into
  canonical order. If `True` will use the default order, otherwise if a
  string this specifies a custom order.

optimize

: The contraction path optimizer to use.

sweep_reverse

: Whether to sweep in the reverse direction, resulting in a left
  canonical form instead of right canonical.

equalize_norms

: Whether to renormalize the tensors during the compression procedure.
  If `True` the gathered exponent will be redistributed equally among
  the tensors. If a float, all tensors will be renormalized to this
  value, and the gathered exponent is tracked in `tn.exponent` of the
  returned tensor network.

inplace

: Whether to perform the compression inplace or not.

compress_opts

: Supplied to {func}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_split`.

# Returns

TensorNetwork

: The compressed tensor network, with canonical center at
  `site_tags[0]` ('right canonical' form) or `site_tags[-1]` ('left
  canonical' form) if `sweep_reverse`.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_1d_compress.tensor_network_1d_compress_dm
documentation:
Compress any 1D-like tensor network using the 'density matrix' method
(<https://tensornetwork.org/mps/algorithms/denmat_mpo_mps/>).

While this has the same scaling as the direct method, in practice it can
often be faster, especially at large bond dimensions. Potentially there are
some situations where the direct method is more stable with regard to
precision, since the density matrix method works in the 'squared' picture.

# Parameters

tn

: The tensor network to compress. Every tensor should have exactly one of
  the site tags. Each site can have multiple tensors and output indices.

max_bond

: The maximum bond dimension to compress to.

cutoff

: The truncation error to use when compressing the double layer tensor
  network.

site_tags

: The tags to use to group and order the tensors from `tn`. If not
  given, uses `tn.site_tags`. The tensor network built will have one
  tensor per site, in the order given by `site_tags`.

normalize

: Whether to normalize the final tensor network, making use of the fact
  that the output tensor network is in right canonical form.

cutoff_mode

: The mode to use when truncating the singular values of the decomposed
  tensors. See {func}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_split`. Note for the density
  matrix method the default 'rsum1' mode acts like 'rsum2' for the direct
  method due to truncating in the squared space.

permute_arrays

: Whether to permute the array indices of the final tensor network into
  canonical order. If `True` will use the default order, otherwise if a
  string this specifies a custom order.

optimize

: The contraction path optimizer to use.

sweep_reverse

: Whether to sweep in the reverse direction, resulting in a left
  canonical form instead of right canonical.

canonize

: Dummy argument to match the signature of other compression methods.

equalize_norms

: Whether to equalize the norms of the tensors after compression. If an
  explicit value is give, then the norms will be set to that value, and
  the overall scaling factor will be accumulated into `.exponent`.

inplace

: Whether to perform the compression inplace or not.

compress_opts

: Supplied to {func}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_split`.

# Returns

TensorNetwork

: The compressed tensor network, with canonical center at
  `site_tags[0]` ('right canonical' form) or `site_tags[-1]` ('left
  canonical' form) if `sweep_reverse`.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_1d_compress.tensor_network_1d_compress_zipup
documentation:
Compress a 1D-like tensor network using the 'zip-up' algorithm due to
'Minimally Entangled Typical Thermal State Algorithms', E.M. Stoudenmire &
Steven R. White (<https://arxiv.org/abs/1002.1305>). The returned tensor
network will have one tensor per site, in the order given by `site_tags`,
with canonical center at `site_tags[0]` ('right' canonical form).

The zipup algorithm scales better than the direct and density matrix
methods when multiple tensors are present at each site (such as MPO-MPS
multiplication), but is less accurate due to the compressions taking place
in an only pseudo-canonical gauge. It generally also only makes sense in
the fixed bond dimension case, as opposed to relying on a specific
`cutoff` only.

# Parameters

tn

: The tensor network to compress. Every tensor should have exactly one of
  the site tags. Each site can have multiple tensors and output indices.

max_bond

: The maximum bond dimension to compress to.

cutoff

: A dynamic threshold for discarding singular values when compressing.

site_tags

: The tags to use to group and order the tensors from `tn`. If not
  given, uses `tn.site_tags`. The tensor network built will have one
  tensor per site, in the order given by `site_tags`.

canonize

: Whether to pseudo canonicalize the initial tensor network.

normalize

: Whether to normalize the final tensor network, making use of the fact
  that the output tensor network is in right canonical form.

cutoff_mode

: The mode to use when truncating the singular values of the decomposed
  tensors. See {func}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_split`.

permute_arrays

: Whether to permute the array indices of the final tensor network into
  canonical order. If `True` will use the default order, otherwise if a
  string this specifies a custom order.

optimize

: The contraction path optimizer to use.

sweep_reverse

: Whether to sweep in the reverse direction, resulting in a left
  canonical form instead of right canonical.

equalize_norms

: Whether to equalize the norms of the tensors after compression. If an
  explicit value is give, then the norms will be set to that value, and
  the overall scaling factor will be accumulated into `.exponent`.

inplace

: Whether to perform the compression inplace or not.

compress_opts

: Supplied to {func}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_split`.

# Returns

TensorNetwork

: The compressed tensor network, with canonical center at
  `site_tags[0]` ('right canonical' form) or `site_tags[-1]` ('left
  canonical' form) if `sweep_reverse`.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_1d_compress.tensor_network_1d_compress_zipup_first
documentation:
Compress this 1D-like tensor network using the 'zip-up first' algorithm,
that is, first compressing the tensor network to a larger bond dimension
using the 'zip-up' algorithm, then compressing to the desired bond
dimension using a direct sweep.

Depending on the value of `max_bond` and `max_bond_zipup`, this can be
scale better than the direct and density matrix methods, but reach close to
the same accuracy. As with the 'zip-up' method, there is no advantage
unless there are multiple tensors per site, and it generally only makes
sense in the fixed bond dimension case, as opposed to relying on a
specific `cutoff` only.

# Parameters

tn

: The tensor network to compress. Every tensor should have exactly one of
  the site tags. Each site can have multiple tensors and output indices.

max_bond

: The final maximum bond dimension to compress to.

max_bond_zipup

: The intermediate maximum bond dimension to compress to using the
  'zip-up' algorithm. If not given and `max_bond` is, this is set as
  twice the target bond dimension, `2 * max_bond`.

cutoff

: A dynamic threshold for discarding singular values when compressing.

cutoff_zipup

: A dynamic threshold for discarding singular values when compressing to
  the intermediate bond dimension using the 'zip-up' algorithm. If not
  given, this is set to the same as `cutoff` if a maximum bond is
  given, else `cutoff / 10`.

site_tags

: The tags to use to group and order the tensors from `tn`. If not
  given, uses `tn.site_tags`. The tensor network built will have one
  tensor per site, in the order given by `site_tags`.

canonize

: Whether to pseudo canonicalize the initial tensor network.

normalize

: Whether to normalize the final tensor network, making use of the fact
  that the output tensor network is in right canonical form.

cutoff_mode

: The mode to use when truncating the singular values of the decomposed
  tensors. See {func}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_split`.

permute_arrays

: Whether to permute the array indices of the final tensor network into
  canonical order. If `True` will use the default order, otherwise if a
  string this specifies a custom order.

optimize

: The contraction path optimizer to use.

sweep_reverse

: Whether to sweep in the reverse direction, resulting in a left
  canonical form instead of right canonical.

equalize_norms

: Whether to equalize the norms of the tensors after compression. If an
  explicit value is give, then the norms will be set to that value, and
  the overall scaling factor will be accumulated into `.exponent`.

inplace

: Whether to perform the compression inplace or not.

compress_opts

: Supplied to {func}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_split`.

# Returns

TensorNetwork

: The compressed tensor network, with canonical center at
  `site_tags[0]` ('right canonical' form) or `site_tags[-1]` ('left
  canonical' form) if `sweep_reverse`.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_1d_compress._tn1d_fit_sum_sweep_1site
documentation: Core sweep of the 1-site 1D fit algorithm.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_1d_compress._tn1d_fit_sum_sweep_2site
documentation: Core sweep of the 2-site 1D fit algorithm.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_1d_compress.tensor_network_1d_compress_fit
documentation:
Compress any 1D-like (can have multiple tensors per site) tensor network
or sum of tensor networks to an exactly 1D (one tensor per site) tensor
network of bond dimension `max_bond` using the 1-site or 2-site variational
fitting (or 'DMRG-style') method. The tensor network(s) can have arbitrary
inner and outer structure.

This method has the lowest scaling of the standard 1D compression methods
and can also provide the most accurate compression, but the actual speed
and accuracy depend on the number of iterations required and initial guess,
making it a more 'hands-on' method.

It's also the only method to support fitting to a sum of tensor networks
directly, rather than having to forming the explicitly summed TN first.

# Parameters

tns

: The tensor network or tensor networks to compress. Each tensor network
  should have the same outer index structure, and within each tensor
  network every tensor should have exactly one of the site tags.

max_bond

: The maximum bond dimension to compress to. If not given, this is set
  as the maximum bond dimension of the initial guess tensor network, if
  any, else infinite for `bsz=2`.

cutoff

: A dynamic threshold for discarding singular values when compressing.
  This is only relevant for the 2-site sweeping algorithm (`bsz=2`),
  where it defaults to 1e-10.

tn_fit

: An initial guess for the compressed tensor network. It should matching
  outer indices and site tags with `tn`. If a `dict`, this is assumed
  to be options to supply to `tensor_network_1d_compress` to construct
  the initial guess, inheriting various defaults like `initial_bond_dim`.
  If a string, e.g. `"zipup"`, this is shorthand for that compression
  method with default settings. If not given, a random 1D tensor network
  will be used.

bsz

: The size of the block to optimize while sweeping. If `"auto"`, this
  will be inferred from the value of `max_bond` and `cutoff`.

initial_bond_dim

: The initial bond dimension to use when creating the initial guess. This
  is only relevant if `tn_fit` is not given. For each sweep the allowed
  bond dimension is doubled, up to `max_bond`. For 1-site this occurs
  via explicit bond expansion, while for 2-site it occurs during the
  2-site tensor decomposition.

max_iterations

: The maximum number of variational sweeps to perform.

tol

: The convergence tolerance, in terms of local tensor distance
  normalized. If zero, there will be exactly `max_iterations` sweeps.

site_tags

: The tags to use to group and order the tensors from `tn`. If not
  given, uses `tn.site_tags`. The tensor network built will have one
  tensor per site, in the order given by `site_tags`.

cutoff_mode

: The mode to use when truncating the singular values of the decomposed
  tensors. See {func}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_split`, if using the 2-site
  sweeping algorithm.

sweep_sequence

: The sequence of sweeps to perform, e.g. `"LR"` means first sweep left
  to right, then right to left. The sequence is cycled.

normalize

: Whether to normalize the final tensor network, making use of the fact
  that the output tensor network is in left or right canonical form.

permute_arrays

: Whether to permute the array indices of the final tensor network into
  canonical order. If `True` will use the default order, otherwise if a
  string this specifies a custom order.

optimize

: The contraction path optimizer to use.

canonize

: Dummy argument to match the signature of other compression methods.

sweep_reverse

: Whether to sweep in the reverse direction, swapping whether the final
  tensor network is in right or left canonical form, which also depends
  on the last sweep direction.

equalize_norms

: Whether to equalize the norms of the tensors after compression. If an
  explicit value is give, then the norms will be set to that value, and
  the overall scaling factor will be accumulated into `.exponent`.

inplace_fit

: Whether to perform the compression inplace on the initial guess tensor
  network, `tn_fit`, if supplied.

inplace

: Whether to perform the compression inplace on the target tensor network
  supplied, or `tns[0]` if a sequence to sum is supplied.

progbar

: Whether to show a progress bar. Note the progress bar shows the maximum
  change of any single tensor norm, *not* the global change in norm or
  truncation error.

compress_opts

: Supplied to {func}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_split`, if using the 2-site
  sweeping algorithm.

# Returns

TensorNetwork

: The compressed tensor network. Depending on `sweep_reverse` and the
  last sweep direction, the canonical center will be at either L:
  `site_tags[0]` or R: `site_tags[-1]`, or the opposite if
  `sweep_reverse`.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_1d_compress.tensor_network_1d_compress_fit_guess
documentation:
Compress any 1D-like (can have multiple tensors per site) tensor network
to an exactly 1D (one tensor per site) tensor network of bond dimension
`max_bond` using by default 1-site variational fitting (or 'DMRG-style')
method starting with a non-random guess tensor network, e.g. from the cheap
zip-up or projector methods.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_1d_compress.tensor_network_1d_compress
documentation:
Compress a 1D-like tensor network using the specified method.

# Parameters

tn

: The tensor network to compress. Every tensor should have exactly one of
  the site tags. Each site can have multiple tensors and output indices.

max_bond

: The maximum bond dimension to compress to.

cutoff

: A dynamic threshold for discarding singular values when compressing.

method

: The compression method to use.

site_tags

: The tags to use to group and order the tensors from `tn`. If not
  given, uses `tn.site_tags`. The tensor network built will have one
  tensor per site, in the order given by `site_tags`.

canonize

: Whether to perform canonicalization, pseudo or otherwise depending on
  the method, before compressing. Ignored for `method='dm'` and
  `method='fit'`.

permute_arrays

: Whether to permute the array indices of the final tensor network into
  canonical order. If `True` will use the default order, otherwise if a
  string this specifies a custom order.

optimize

: The contraction path optimizer to use.

sweep_reverse

: Whether to sweep in the reverse direction, resulting in a left
  canonical form instead of right canonical (for the fit method, this
  also depends on the last sweep direction).

equalize_norms

: Whether to equalize the norms of the tensors after compression. If an
  explicit value is give, then the norms will be set to that value, and
  the overall scaling factor will be accumulated into `.exponent`.

inplace

: Whether to perform the compression inplace.

kwargs

: Supplied to the chosen compression method.

# Returns

TensorNetwork

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_1d_compress.mps_gate_with_mpo_lazy
documentation:
Apply an MPO to an MPS lazily, i.e. nothing is contracted, but the new
TN object has the same outer indices as the original MPS.
--------------- MPO-MPS gating using 1D compression methods --------------- #

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_1d_compress.mps_gate_with_mpo_direct
documentation:
Apply an MPO to an MPS using the boundary compression method, that is,
explicitly contracting site-wise to form a MPS-like TN, canonicalizing in
one direction, then compressing in the other. This has the same scaling as
the density matrix (dm) method, but a larger prefactor. It can still be
faster for small bond dimensions however, and is potentially higher
precision since it works in the space of singular values directly rather
than singular values squared. It is not quite optimal in terms of error due
to the compounding errors of the SVDs.

# Parameters

mps

: The MPS to gate.

mpo

: The MPO to gate with.

max_bond

: The maximum bond dimension to compress to.

cutoff

: A dynamic threshold for discarding singular values when compressing.

compress_opts

: Supplied to {func}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_split`.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_1d_compress.mps_gate_with_mpo_dm
documentation:
Gate this MPS with an MPO, using the density matrix compression method.

# Parameters

mps

: The MPS to gate.

mpo

: The MPO to gate with.

max_bond

: The maximum bond dimension to keep when compressing the double layer
  tensor network, if any.

cutoff

: The truncation error to use when compressing the double layer tensor
  network, if any.

compress_opts

: Supplied to {func}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_split`.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_1d_compress.mps_gate_with_mpo_zipup
documentation:
Apply an MPO to an MPS using the 'zip-up' algorithm due to
'Minimally Entangled Typical Thermal State Algorithms', E.M. Stoudenmire &
Steven R. White (<https://arxiv.org/abs/1002.1305>).

# Parameters

mps

: The MPS to gate.

mpo

: The MPO to gate with.

max_bond

: The maximum bond dimension to compress to.

cutoff

: A dynamic threshold for discarding singular values when compressing.

site_tags

: The tags to use to group and order the tensors from `tn`. If not
  given, uses `tn.site_tags`. The tensor network built will have one
  tensor per site, in the order given by `site_tags`.

canonize

: Whether to pseudo canonicalize the initial tensor network.

normalize

: Whether to normalize the final tensor network, making use of the fact
  that the output tensor network is in right canonical form.

permute_arrays

: Whether to permute the array indices of the final tensor network into
  canonical order. If `True` will use the default order, otherwise if a
  string this specifies a custom order.

optimize

: The contraction path optimizer to use.

compress_opts

: Supplied to {func}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_split`.

# Returns

MatrixProductState

: The compressed MPS, in right canonical form.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_1d_compress.mps_gate_with_mpo_zipup_first
documentation:
Apply an MPO to an MPS by first using the zip-up method with a larger
bond dimension, then doing a regular compression sweep to the target final
bond dimension. This avoids forming an intermediate MPS with bond dimension
`mps.max_bond() * mpo.max_bond()`.

# Parameters

mps

: The MPS to gate.

mpo

: The MPO to gate with.

max_bond

: The target final bond dimension.

max_bond_zipup

: The maximum bond dimension to use when zip-up compressing the double
  layer tensor network. If not given, defaults to `2 * max_bond`.
  Needs to be smaller than `mpo.max_bond()` for any savings.

cutoff

: The truncation error to use when performing the final regular
  compression sweep.

cutoff_zipup

: The truncation error to use when performing the zip-up compression.

canonize

: Whether to pseudo canonicalize the initial tensor network.

optimize

: The contraction path optimizer to use.

compress_opts

: Supplied to {func}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_split` (both the zip-up and
  final sweep).

# Returns

MatrixProductState

: The compressed MPS, in right canonical form.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_1d_compress.mps_gate_with_mpo_fit
documentation:
Gate an MPS with an MPO using the variational fitting or DMRG-style
method.

Parameters
----------
mps : MatrixProductState
    The MPS to gate.
mpo : MatrixProductOperator
    The MPO to gate with.
max_bond : int
    The maximum bond dimension to compress to.

Returns
-------
MatrixProductState
    The gated MPS.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_1d_compress.mps_gate_with_mpo_autofit
documentation:
Fit a MPS to a MPO applied to an MPS using geometry generic versions
of either ALS or autodiff. This is usually much less efficient that using
the 1D specific methods.

Some nice alternatives to the default fit_opts:

    - method="autodiff"
    - method="als", solver="lstsq"

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_1d_compress.mps_gate_with_mpo_projector
documentation:
Apply an MPO to an MPS using local projectors, in the style of CTMRG
or HOTRG, without using information beyond the neighboring 4 tensors.

modules.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_dmrg
documentation: DMRG-like variational algorithms, but in tensor network language.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_dmrg.get_default_opts
documentation:
Get the default advanced settings for DMRG.

# Returns

default_sweep_sequence

: How to sweep. Will be repeated, e.g. "RRL" -> RRLRRLRRL..., default: R.

bond_compress_method

: Method used to compress sites after update.

bond_compress_cutoff_mode

: How to perform compression truncation.

bond_expand_rand_strength

: In DMRG1, strength of randomness to expand bonds with. Needed to avoid
  singular matrices after expansion.

local_eig_tol

: Relative tolerance to solve inner eigenproblem to, larger = quicker but
  more unstable, default: 1e-3. Note this can be much looser than the
  overall tolerance, the starting point for each local solve is the
  previous state, and the overall accuracy comes from multiple sweeps.

local_eig_ncv

: Number of inner eigenproblem lanczos vectors. Smaller can mean quicker.

local_eig_backend

: Which to backend to use for the inner eigenproblem. None or 'AUTO' to
  choose best. Generally `'SLEPC'` best if available for large
  problems, but it can't currently handle `LinearOperator` Neff as well
  as `'lobpcg'`.

local_eig_maxiter

: Maximum number of inner eigenproblem iterations.

local_eig_ham_dense

: Force dense representation of the effective hamiltonian.

local_eig_EPSType

: Eigensovler tpye if `local_eig_backend='slepc'`.

local_eig_norm_dense

: Force dense representation of the effective norm.

periodic_segment_size

: How large (as a proportion if float) to make the 'segments' in periodic
  DMRG. During a sweep everything outside this (the 'long way round') is
  compressed so the effective energy and norm can be efficiently formed.
  Tradeoff: longer segments means having to compress less, but also
  having a shorter 'long way round', meaning that it needs a larger bond
  to represent it and can be 'pseudo-orthogonalized' less effectively.
  0.5 is the largest fraction that makes sense. Set to >= 1.0 to not
  use segmentation at all, which is better for small systems.

periodic_compress_method

: Which method to perform the transfer matrix compression with.

periodic_compress_norm_eps

: Precision to compress the norm transfer matrix in periodic systems.

periodic_compress_ham_eps

: Precision to compress the energy transfer matrix in periodic systems.

periodic_compress_max_bond

: The maximum bond to use when compressing transfer matrices.

periodic_nullspace_fudge_factor

: Factor to add to `Heff` and `Neff` to remove nullspace.

periodic_canonize_inv_tol

: When psuedo-orthogonalizing, an inverse gauge is generated that can be
  very ill-conditioned. This factor controls cutting off the small
  singular values of the gauge to stop this.

periodic_orthog_tol

: When psuedo-orthogonalizing, if the local norm is within this
  distance to 1 (pseudo-orthogonoalized), then the generalized eigen
  decomposition is *not* used, which is much more efficient. If set too
  large the total normalization can become unstable.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_dmrg.get_cyclic_canonizer
documentation:
Get a function to use as a callback for `MovingEnvironment` that
approximately orthogonalizes the segments of periodic MPS.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_dmrg.parse_2site_inds_dims
documentation:
Sort out the dims and inds of:

```
---O---O---
   |   |
```

For use in 2 site algorithms.
DMRG Base                                  #

classes.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_dmrg.MovingEnvironment

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_dmrg.MovingEnvironment.init_segment
documentation:
Initialize the environments in `range(start, stop)` so that one
can start sweeping from the side defined by `begin`.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_dmrg.MovingEnvironment.init_non_segment
documentation:
Compress and label the effective env not in `range(start, stop)`
if cyclic, else just add some dummy left and right end pieces.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_dmrg.MovingEnvironment.move_to
documentation: Move this effective environment to site `i`.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_dmrg.MovingEnvironment.__call__
documentation: Get the current environment.

Helper class for efficiently moving the effective 'environment' of a
few sites in a 1D tensor network. E.g. for `begin='left', bsz=2`, this
initializes the right environments like so:

```
n - 1: ●─●─●─     ─●─●─●
       │ │ │       │ │ │
       H─H─H─ ... ─H─H─H
       │ │ │       │ │ │
       ●─●─●─     ─●─●─●

n - 2: ●─●─●─     ─●─●─╮
       │ │ │       │ │ ●
       H─H─H─ ... ─H─H─H
       │ │ │       │ │ ●
       ●─●─●─     ─●─●─╯

n - 3: ●─●─●─     ─●─╮
       │ │ │       │ ●●
       H─H─H─ ... ─H─HH
       │ │ │       │ ●●
       ●─●─●─     ─●─╯

...

0    : ●─●─╮
       │ │ ●●   ●●●
       H─H─HH...HHH
       │ │ ●●   ●●●
       ●─●─╯
```

which can then be used to efficiently generate the left environments as
each site is updated. For example if `bsz=2` and the environements have
been shifted many sites into the middle, then `MovingEnvironment()`
returns something like:

```
     <---> bsz sites
    ╭─●─●─╮
●●●●● │ │ ●●●●●●●
HHHHH─H─H─HHHHHHH
●●●●● │ │ ●●●●●●●
    ╰─●─●─╯
0 ... i i+1 ... n-1
```

For periodic systems `MovingEnvironment` approximates the 'long
way round' transfer matrices. E.g consider replacing segment B
(to arbitrary precision) with an SVD:

```
╭───────────────────────────────────────────────╮
╰─A─A─A─A─A─A─A─A─A─A─A─A─B─B─B─B─B─B─B─B─B─B─B─╯
  │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │           ==>
╭─A─A─A─A─A─A─A─A─A─A─A─A─B─B─B─B─B─B─B─B─B─B─B─╮
╰───────────────────────────────────────────────╯

╭┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄╮
┊   ╭─A─A─A─A─A─A─A─A─A─A─A─A─╮   ┊                       ==>
╰┄<BL │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ BR>┄╯
    ╰─A─A─A─A─A─A─A─A─A─A─A─A─╯
      ^                     ^
segment_start          segment_stop - 1

╭┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄╮
┊   ╭─A─A─╮                        ┊                      ==>
╰┄<BL │ │ AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAABR>┄╯
    ╰─A─A─╯
      ...
    <-bsz->

╭┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄╮
┊               ╭─A─A─╮           ┊                       ==>
╰~<BLAAAAAAAAAAA  │ │ AAAAAAAABR>~╯
                ╰─A─A─╯
                  i i+1
     -----sweep--------->
```

Can then contract and store left and right environments for efficient
sweeping just as in non-periodic case. If the segment is long enough (50+)
sites, often only 1 singular value is needed, and thus the efficiency is
the same as for OBC.

# Parameters

tn

: The 1D tensor network, should be closed, i.e. an overlap of some sort.

begin

: Which side to start at and sweep from.

bsz

: The number of sites that form the the 'non-environment',
  e.g. 2 for DMRG2.

ssz

: The size of the segment to use, if float, the proportion. Default: 1/2.

eps

: The tolerance to approximate the transfer matrix with. See
  {meth}`~quimb.tensor.TensorNetwork.replace_with_svd`.

cyclic

: Whether this is a periodic `MovingEnvironment`.

segment_callbacks

: Functions with signature `callback(start, stop, self.begin)`, to be
  called every time a new segment is initialized.

method

: How to perform the transfer matrix compression if PBC. See
  {meth}`~quimb.tensor.TensorNetwork.replace_with_svd`.

max_bond

: If > 0, the maximum bond of the compressed transfer matrix.

norm

: If True, treat this `MovingEnvironment` as the state overlap, which
  enables a few extra checks.

# Notes

Does not necessarily need to be an operator overlap tensor network. Useful
for any kind of sweep where only local tensor updates are being made. Note
that *only* the current site is completely up-to-date and can be modified
with changes meant to propagate.

Compress a site having updated it. Also serves to move the

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_dmrg.DMRG._eigs
documentation: Find single eigenpair, using all the internal settings.

documentation: Construct the effective Hamiltonian, and if needed, norm.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_dmrg.DMRG._update_local_state_1site
documentation:
Find the single site effective tensor groundstate of:

```
>->->->->-/|\-<-<-<-<-<-<-<-<          /|\       <-- uix
| | | | |  |  | | | | | | | |         / | \
H-H-H-H-H--H--H-H-H-H-H-H-H-H   =    L--H--R
| | | | | i|  | | | | | | | |         \i| /
>->->->->-\|/-<-<-<-<-<-<-<-<          \|/       <-- lix
```

And insert it back into the states `k` and `b`, and thus
`TN_energy`.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_dmrg.DMRG._update_local_state_2site
documentation:
Find the 2-site effective tensor groundstate of:

```
>->->->->-/| |\-<-<-<-<-<-<-<-<          /| |\
| | | | |  | |  | | | | | | | |         / | | \
H-H-H-H-H--H-H--H-H-H-H-H-H-H-H   =    L--H-H--R
| | | | |  i i+1| | | | | | | |         \ | | /
>->->->->-\| |/-<-<-<-<-<-<-<-<          \| |/
                                     i i+1
```

And insert it back into the states `k` and `b`, and thus
`TN_energy`.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_dmrg.DMRG._update_local_state
documentation: Move envs to site `i` and dispatch to the correct local updater.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_dmrg.DMRG.sweep
documentation:
Perform a sweep of optimizations, either rightwards:

```
  optimize -->
    ...
>->-o-<-<-<-<-<-<-<-<-<-<-<-<-<
| | | | | | | | | | | | | | | |
H-H-H-H-H-H-H-H-H-H-H-H-H-H-H-H
| | | | | | | | | | | | | | | |
>->-o-<-<-<-<-<-<-<-<-<-<-<-<-<
```

or leftwards (`direction='L'`):

```
                <-- optimize
                          ...
>->->->->->->->->->->->->-o-<-<
| | | | | | | | | | | | | | | |
H-H-H-H-H-H-H-H-H-H-H-H-H-H-H-H
| | | | | | | | | | | | | | | |
>->->->->->->->->->->->->-o-<-<
```

After the sweep the state is left or right canonized respectively.

# Parameters

direction

: Sweep from left to right (->) or right to left (\<-) respectively.

canonize

: Canonize the state first, not needed if doing alternate sweeps.

verbosity

: Show a progress bar for the sweep.

update_opts :

: Supplied to `self._update_local_state`.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_dmrg.DMRG._print_pre_sweep
documentation: Print this before each sweep.
----------------- overloadable 'plugin' style methods ----------------- #

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_dmrg.DMRG._compute_post_sweep
documentation: Compute this after each sweep.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_dmrg.DMRG._print_post_sweep
documentation: Print this after each sweep.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_dmrg.DMRG._check_convergence
documentation: By default check the absolute change in energy.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_dmrg.DMRG.solve
documentation:
Solve the system with a sequence of sweeps, up to a certain
absolute tolerance in the energy or maximum number of sweeps.

# Parameters

tol

: The absolute tolerance to converge energy to.

bond_dims

: Overide the initial/current bond_dim sequence.

cutoffs

: Overide the initial/current cutoff sequence.

sweep_sequence

: String made of 'L' and 'R' defining the sweep sequence, e.g 'RRL'.
  The sequence will be repeated until `max_sweeps` is reached.

max_sweeps

: The maximum number of sweeps to perform.

verbosity

: How much information to print about progress.

suppress_warnings

: Whether to suppress warnings about non-convergence, usually due to
  the intentional low accuracy of the inner eigensolve.

# Returns

converged

: Whether the algorithm has converged to `tol` yet.

Density Matrix Renormalization Group variational groundstate search.
Some initialising arguments act as defaults, but can be overidden with
each solve or sweep. See {func}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_dmrg.get_default_opts`
for the list of advanced options initialized in the `opts` attribute.

# Parameters

ham

: The hamiltonian in MPO form.

bond_dims

: The bond-dimension of the MPS to optimize. If `bsz > 1`, then this
  corresponds to the maximum bond dimension when splitting the effective
  local groundstate. If a sequence is supplied then successive sweeps
  iterate through, then repeate the final value. E.g.
  `[16, 32, 64] -> (16, 32, 64, 64, 64, ...)`.

cutoffs

: The cutoff threshold(s) to use when compressing. If a sequence is
  supplied then successive sweeps iterate through, then repeate the final
  value. E.g. `[1e-5, 1e-7, 1e-9] -> (1e-5, 1e-7, 1e-9, 1e-9, ...)`.

bsz

: Number of sites to optimize for locally i.e. DMRG1 or DMRG2.

which

: Whether to search for smallest or largest real part eigenvectors.

p0

: If given, use as the initial state.

# Attributes

state

: The current, optimized state.

energy

: The current most optimized energy.

energies

: The total energy after each sweep.

local_energies

: The local energies per sweep: `local_energies[i, j]` contains the
  local energy found at the jth step of the (i+1)th sweep.

total_energies

: The total energies per sweep: `local_energies[i, j]` contains the
  total energy after the jth step of the (i+1)th sweep.

opts

: Advanced options e.g. relating to the inner eigensolve or compression,
  see {func}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_dmrg.get_default_opts`.

(bond_sizes_ham)

: If cyclic, the sizes of the energy environement transfer matrix bonds,
  per segment, per sweep.

(bond_sizes_norm)

: If cyclic, the sizes of the norm environement transfer matrix bonds,
  per segment, per sweep.

classes.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_dmrg.DMRGX

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_dmrg.DMRGX._update_local_state_1site_dmrgx
documentation:
Like `_update_local_state`, but re-insert all eigenvectors, then
choose the one with best overlap with `eff_ovlp`.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_dmrg.DMRGX._update_local_state
documentation: def _update_local_state_2site_dmrgx(self, i, direction, **compress_opts):
raise NotImplementedError("2-site DMRGX not implemented yet.")
dims, lix_L, lix_R, lix, uix_L, uix_R, uix, l_bond_ind, u_bond_ind =\
parse_2site_inds_dims(self._k, self._b, i)
# contract remaining hamiltonian and get its dense representation
eff_ham = (self._eff_ham ^ '_HAM')['_HAM']
eff_ham.fuse_((('lower', lix), ('upper', uix)))
A = eff_ham.data
# eigen-decompose and reshape eigenvectors thus::
#    EE
D = prod(dims)
if D <= self.opts['local_eig_partial_cutoff']:
evals, evecs = eigh(A)
else:
if isinstance(self.opts['local_eig_partial_k'], float):
k = int(self.opts['local_eig_partial_k'] * D)
else:
k = self.opts['local_eig_partial_k']
# find the 2-site local state using previous as initial guess
v0 = self._k[i].contract(self._k[i + 1], output_inds=uix).data
evals, evecs = eigh(
A, sigma=self.energies[-1], v0=v0,
k=k, tol=self.opts['local_eig_tol'], backend='scipy')

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_dmrg.DMRGX.sweep
documentation:
Perform a sweep of the algorithm.

# Parameters

direction

: Sweep from left to right (->) or right to left (\<-) respectively.

canonize

: Canonize the state first, not needed if doing alternate sweeps.

verbosity

: Show a progress bar for the sweep.

update_opts :

: Supplied to `self._update_local_state`.

Class implmenting DMRG-X [1], whereby local effective energy eigenstates
are chosen to maximise overlap with the previous step's state, leading to
convergence on an mid-spectrum eigenstate of the full hamiltonian, as long
as it is perturbatively close to the original state.

[1] Khemani, V., Pollmann, F. & Sondhi, S. L. Obtaining Highly Excited
Eigenstates of Many-Body Localized Hamiltonians by the Density Matrix
Renormalization Group Approach. Phys. Rev. Lett. 116, 247204 (2016).

# Parameters

ham

: The hamiltonian in MPO form, should have ~area-law eigenstates.

p0

: The initial MPS guess, e.g. a computation basis state.

bond_dims

: See {class}`DMRG`.

cutoffs

: See {class}`DMRG`.

# Attributes

k

: The current, optimized state.

energies

: The list of energies after each sweep.
DMRGX                                    #

modules.name: quimb.tensor.circuit_gen
documentation: Functions for generating specific, e.g. ansatz, circuits.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.circuit_gen.inject_u3s
documentation:
Take a sequence of pairs denoting qubits to entangle and interleave one
single qubit gate inbetween every leg. For example:

> ent_gates = [(0, 1), (2, 3), (1, 2)]

Would go get made into a circuit like:

```
|  |  |  |        |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |        |  u  u  |
|  |  |  |        |  |  |  |
|  o++o  |        u  o++o  u
|  |  |  |        |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  -->   |  u  u  |
|  |  |  |        |  |  |  |
o++o  o++o        o++o  o++o
|  |  |  |        |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |        u  u  u  u
|  |  |  |        |  |  |  |
```

Technically, this generates a bipartite graph between single qubit and two
qubit tensors, and should be the most expressive circuit possible for that
'budget' of entangling gates.

# Parameters

ent_gates

: A 'stack' of entangling gate pairs to apply.

gate2

: The gate to use for the entanling pairs.

avoid_doubling

: Whether to avoid placing an entangling gate directly above the same
  entangling gate (there will still be single qubit gates interleaved).

# Returns

Circuit

functions.name: quimb.tensor.circuit_gen.gates_to_param_circuit
documentation:
Turn the sequence `gates` into a `Circuit` of `n` qubits, with any
gates that appear in `parametrize` being... parametrized.

# Parameters

gates

: The gates describing the circuit.

n

: The number of qubits to make the circuit act one.

parametrize

: Which gates to parametrize.

circuit_opts

: Supplied to {class}`~quimb.tensor.circuit.Circuit`.

# Returns

Circuit

A 1D circuit ansatz with forward and backward layers of entangling

```
|  |  |  |
u  u  |  |
o++o  u  |
|  |  |  u
|  o++o  |
|  |  u  |
|  |  o++o
u  u  u  u
|  |  o++o
|  |  u  |
|  o++o  |
|  u  |  u
o++o  u  |
u  u  |  |
|  |  |  |
```

# Parameters

n

: The number of qubits.

depth

: The number of entangling gates per pair.

gate2

: The gate to use for the entanling pairs.

seed

: Random seed for parameters.

opts

: Supplied to {func}`~quimb.tensor.circuit_gen.gates_to_param_circuit`.

# See Also

circ_ansatz_1D_rand, circ_ansatz_1D_brickwork

functions.name: quimb.tensor.circuit_gen.circ_ansatz_1D_brickwork
documentation:
A 1D circuit ansatz with odd and even layers of entangling
gates interleaved with U3 single qubit unitaries:

```

|  u  u  u  u
u  o++o  o++o
|  u  u  u  |
o++o  o++o  u
|  u  u  u  |
u  o++o  o++o
|  u  u  u  |
o++o  o++o  u
|  u  u  u  u
u  o++o  o++o
|  u  u  u  |
o++o  o++o  u
u  u  u  u  |
|  |  |  |  |
```

# Parameters

n

functions.name: quimb.tensor.circuit_gen.circ_qaoa
documentation:
Generate the QAOA circuit for weighted graph described by `terms`.

$$
|{\bar{\gamma}, \bar{\beta}}\rangle = U_B (\beta _p)
U_C (\gamma _p) \cdots U_B (\beta _1) U_C (\gamma _1) |{+}\rangle
$$

with

$$
U_C (\gamma) = e^{-i \gamma \mathcal{C}} = \prod \limits_{i, j
\in E(G)} e^{-i \gamma w_{i j} Z_i Z_j}
$$

and

$$
U_B (\beta) = \prod \limits_{i \in G} e^{-i \beta X_i}
$$

# Parameters

terms

: The mapping of integer pair keys `(i, j)` to the edge weight values,
  `wij`. The integers should be a contiguous range enumerated from
  zero, with the total number of qubits being inferred from this.

depth

: The number of layers of gates to apply, `p` above.

gammas

: The interaction angles for each layer.

betas

: The rotation angles for each layer.

circuit_opts

: Supplied to {class}`~quimb.tensor.circuit.Circuit`. Note
  `gate_opts={'contract': False}` is set by default (it can be
  overridden) since the RZZ gate, even though it has a rank-2
  decomposition, is also diagonal.

modules.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_mera
documentation: Tools for building and computing with MERA.

classes.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_mera.MERA

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_mera.MERA.rand_invar
documentation: Generate a random translational and scale invariant MERA.

The Multi-scale Entanglement Renormalization Ansatz (MERA) state:

```
    ...     ...     ...     ...     ...     ...
 |       |       |       |       |       |       |
ISO     ISO     ISO     ISO     ISO     ISO     ISO   :
   \   /   \   /   \   /   \   /   \   /   \   /      : '_LAYER1'
    UNI     UNI     UNI     UNI     UNI     UNI       :
   /   \   /   \   /   \   /   \   /   \   /   \
O ISO ISO ISO ISO ISO ISO ISO ISO ISO ISO ISO ISO I   :
| | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | |   : '_LAYER0'
UNI UNI UNI UNI UNI UNI UNI UNI UNI UNI UNI UNI UNI   :
| | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | |  <-- phys_dim
0 1 2 3 4 ....                            ... L-2 L-1
```

# Parameters

L

: The number of phyiscal sites. Shoule be a power of 2.

uni

: The unitary operator(s). These will be cycled over and placed from
  bottom left to top right in diagram above.

iso

: The isometry operator(s). These will be cycled over and placed from
  bottom left to top right in diagram above.

phys_dim

: The dimension of the local hilbert space.

dangle

: Whether to leave a dangling index on the final isometry, in order to
  maintain perfect scale invariance, else join the final unitaries just
  with an indentity.

modules.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom
documentation: Classes and algorithms related to arbitrary geometry tensor networks.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom.prod
documentation: Product of all elements in `xs`.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom.tensor_network_align
documentation:
Align an arbitrary number of tensor networks in a stack-like geometry:

```
a-a-a-a-a-a-a-a-a-a-a-a-a-a-a-a-a-a
| | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | <- ind_ids[0] (defaults to 1st id)
b-b-b-b-b-b-b-b-b-b-b-b-b-b-b-b-b-b
| | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | <- ind_ids[1]
               ...
| | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | <- ind_ids[-2]
y-y-y-y-y-y-y-y-y-y-y-y-y-y-y-y-y-y
| | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | <- ind_ids[-1]
z-z-z-z-z-z-z-z-z-z-z-z-z-z-z-z-z-z
```

# Parameters

tns

: The TNs to align, should be structured and either effective 'vectors'
  (have a `site_ind_id`) or 'operators' (have a `up_ind_id` and
  `lower_ind_id`).

ind_ids

: String with format specifiers to id each level of sites with. Will be
  automatically generated like `(tns[0].site_ind_id, "__ind_a{}__",
  "__ind_b{}__", ...)` if not given.

inplace

: Whether to modify the input tensor networks inplace.

# Returns

tns_aligned : sequence of TensorNetwork

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom.tensor_network_apply_op_vec
documentation:
Apply a general a general tensor network representing an operator (has
`upper_ind_id` and `lower_ind_id`) to a tensor network representing a
vector (has `site_ind_id`), by contracting each pair of tensors at each
site then compressing the resulting tensor network. How the compression
takes place is determined by the type of tensor network passed in. The
returned tensor network has the same site indices as `x`, and it is
the `lower_ind_id` of `A` that is contracted.

This is like performing `A.to_dense() @ x.to_dense()`, or the transpose
thereof, depending on the value of `which_A`.

# Parameters

A

: The tensor network representing the operator.

x

: The tensor network representing the vector.

which_A

: Whether to contract the lower or upper indices of `A` with the site
  indices of `x`.

contract

: Whether to contract the tensors at each site after applying the
  operator, yielding a single tensor at each site.

fuse_multibonds

: If `contract=True`, whether to fuse any multibonds after contracting
  the tensors at each site.

compress

: Whether to compress the resulting tensor network.

inplace

: Whether to modify `x`, the input vector tensor network inplace.

inplace_A

: Whether to modify `A`, the operator tensor network inplace.

compress_opts

: Options to pass to `tn.compress`, where `tn` is the resulting
  tensor network, if `compress=True`.

# Returns

TensorNetworkGenVector

: The same type as `x`.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom.tensor_network_apply_op_op
documentation:
Apply the operator (has upper and lower site inds) represented by tensor
network `A` to the operator represented by tensor network `B`. The
resulting tensor network has the same upper and lower indices as `B`.
Optionally contract the tensors at each site, fuse any multibonds, and
compress the resulting tensor network.

This is like performing `A.to_dense() @ B.to_dense()`, or various
combinations of tranposes thereof, depending on the values of `which_A`
and `which_B`.

# Parameters

A

: The tensor network representing the operator to apply.

B

: The tensor network representing the target operator.

which_A

: Whether to contract the lower or upper indices of `A`.

which_B

: Whether to contract the lower or upper indices of `B`.

contract

: Whether to contract the tensors at each site after applying the
  operator, yielding a single tensor at each site.

fuse_multibonds

: If `contract=True`, whether to fuse any multibonds after contracting
  the tensors at each site.

compress

: Whether to compress the resulting tensor network.

inplace

: Whether to modify `B`, the target tensor network inplace.

inplace_A

: Whether to modify `A`, the applied operator tensor network inplace.

compress_opts

: Options to pass to `tn.compress`, where `tn` is the resulting
  tensor network, if `compress=True`.

# Returns

TensorNetworkGenOperator

: The same type as `B`.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom.create_lazy_edge_map
documentation:
Given a tensor network, where each tensor is in exactly one group or
'site', compute which sites are connected to each other, without checking
each pair.

# Parameters

tn

: The tensor network to analyze.

site_tags

: Which tags to consider as 'sites', by default uses `tn.site_tags`.

# Returns

edges

: Each key is a sorted pair of tags, which are connected, and the value
  is a list of the indices connecting them.

neighbors

: For each site tag, the other site tags it is connected to.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom.tensor_network_ag_sum
documentation:
Add two tensor networks with arbitrary, but matching, geometries. They
should have the same site tags, with a single tensor per site and sites
connected by a single index only (but the name of this index can differ in
the two TNs).

# Parameters

tna

: The first tensor network to add.

tnb

: The second tensor network to add.

site_tags

: Which tags to consider as 'sites', by default uses `tna.site_tags`.

negate

: Whether to negate the second tensor network before adding.

compress

: Whether to compress the resulting tensor network, by calling the
  `compress` method with the given options.

inplace

: Whether to modify the first tensor network inplace.

# Returns

TensorNetworkGen

: The resulting tensor network.

documentation:
Unified helper function for the various methods that compute many
expectations, possibly in parallel, possibly with a progress bar.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom.TensorNetworkGen.combine
documentation:
Combine this tensor network with another, returning a new tensor
network. If the two are compatible, cast the resulting tensor network
to a {class}`TensorNetworkGen` instance.

# Parameters

other

: The other tensor network to combine with.

virtual

: Whether the new tensor network should copy all the incoming tensors
  (`False`, the default), or view them as virtual (`True`).

check_collisions

: Whether to check for index collisions between the two tensor
  networks before combining them. If `True` (the default), any
  inner indices that clash will be mangled.

# Returns

TensorNetworkGen or TensorNetwork

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom.TensorNetworkGen.gen_site_coos

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom.TensorNetworkGen.sites

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom.TensorNetworkGen._get_site_set

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom.TensorNetworkGen.gen_sites_present
documentation:
Generate the sites which are currently present (e.g. if a local view
of a larger tensor network), based on whether their tags are present.

Examples
--------

    >>> tn = qtn.TN3D_rand(4, 4, 4, 2)
    >>> tn_sub = tn.select_local('I1,2,3', max_distance=1)
    >>> list(tn_sub.gen_sites_present())
    [(0, 2, 3), (1, 1, 3), (1, 2, 2), (1, 2, 3), (1, 3, 3), (2, 2, 3)]

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom.TensorNetworkGen.site_tag_id
documentation: The string specifier for tagging each site of this tensor network.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom.TensorNetworkGen.site_tag
documentation: The name of the tag specifiying the tensor at `site`.

Modify the site tags for all or some tensors in this tensor network
(without changing the `site_tag_id`).

new_id

where

  all sites.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom.TensorNetworkGen.gen_regions_sites
documentation:
Generate sets of sites that represent 'regions' where every node is
connected to at least two other region nodes. This is a simple wrapper
around `TensorNewtork.gen_regions` that works with the sites
rather than `tids`.

# Parameters

max_region_size

: Set the maximum number of tensors that can appear in a region. If
  `None`, wait until any valid region is found and set that as the
  maximum size.

tags

: If supplied, only consider regions containing these tids.

tuple[hashable]

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom.TensorNetworkGen.reset_cached_properties
documentation:
Reset any cached properties, one should call this when changing the
actual geometry of a TN inplace, for example.

methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_arbgeom.TensorNetworkGen.normalize_simple
documentation:
Normalize this network using simple local gauges. After calling
this, any tree-like sub network gauged with `gauges` will have
2-norm 1. Inplace operation on both the tensor network and `gauges`.

# Parameters

gauges

: The gauges to normalize with.

A tensor network which notionally has a single tensor per 'site',
though these could be labelled arbitrarily could also be linked in an
arbitrary geometry by bonds.

Reset any cached properties, one should call this when changing the
