methods.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_2d_tebd.FullUpdate.gate
documentation:
Apply the gate `G` at sites where, using a fitting method that
takes into account the current environment.

Implements the 'Full Update' version of 2D imaginary time evolution,
where each application of a gate is fitted to the current tensors using a
boundary contracted environment.

# Parameters

psi0

: The initial state.

ham

: The Hamtiltonian consisting of local terms.

tau

: The default local exponent, if considered as time real values here
  imply imaginary time.

max_bond

: The maximum bond dimension to keep when applying each gate.

gate_opts

: Supplied to {meth}`quimb.tensor.tensor_2d.TensorNetwork2DVector.gate`,
  in addition to `max_bond`. By default `contract` is set to
  'reduce-split' and `cutoff` is set to `0.0`.

ordering

: How to order the terms, if a string is given then use this as the
  strategy given to
  {meth}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_2d_tebd.LocalHam2D.get_auto_ordering`. An
  explicit list of coordinate pairs can also be given. The default is to
  greedily form an 'edge coloring' based on the sorted list of
  Hamiltonian pair coordinates. If a callable is supplied it will be used
  to generate the ordering before each sweep.

second_order_reflect

: If `True`, then apply each layer of gates in `ordering` forward
  with half the time step, then the same with reverse order.

compute_energy_every

: How often to compute and record the energy. If a positive integer 'n',
  the energy is computed *before* every nth sweep (i.e. including before
  the zeroth).

compute_energy_final

: Whether to compute and record the energy at the end of the sweeps
  regardless of the value of `compute_energy_every`. If you start
  sweeping again then this final energy is the same as the zeroth of the
  next set of sweeps and won't be recomputed.

compute_energy_opts

: Supplied to
  {meth}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_2d.PEPS.compute_local_expectation`. By
  default `max_bond` is set to `max(8, D**2)` where `D` is the
  maximum bond to use for applying the gate, `cutoff` is set to `0.0`
  and `normalized` is set to `True`.

compute_energy_fn

: Supply your own function to compute the energy, it should take the
  `TEBD2D` object as its only argument.

callback

: A custom callback to run after every sweep, it should take the
  `TEBD2D` object as its only argument. If it returns any value
  that boolean evaluates to `True` then terminal the evolution.

progbar

: Whether to show a live progress bar during the evolution.

fit_strategy

: Core method used to fit the gate application.

  > - `'als'`: alternating least squares
  > - `'autodiff-fidelity'`: local fidelity using autodiff

fit_opts

: Advanced options for the gate application fitting functions. Defaults
  are inserted and can be accessed via the `.fit_opts` attribute.

compute_envs_every

: How often to recompute the environments used to the fit the gate
  application:

  > - `'term'`: every gate
  > - `'group'`: every set of commuting gates (the default)
  > - `'sweep'`: every total sweep
  > - int: every `x` number of total sweeps

pre_normalize

: Actively renormalize the state using the computed environments.

condition_tensors

: Whether to actively equalize tensor norms for numerical stability.

condition_balance_bonds

: If and when equalizing tensor norms, whether to also balance bonds as
  an additional conditioning.

contract_optimize

: Contraction path optimizer to use for gate + env + sites contractions.

# Attributes

state

: The current state.

ham

: The Hamiltonian being used to evolve.

energy

: The current of the current state, this will trigger a computation if
  the energy at this iteration hasn't been computed yet.

energies

: The energies that have been computed, if any.

its

: The corresponding sequence of iteration numbers that energies have been
  computed at.

taus

: The corresponding sequence of time steps that energies have been
  computed at.

best

: If `keep_best` was set then the best recorded energy and the
  corresponding state that was computed - keys `'energy'` and
  `'state'` respectively.

fit_opts

: Detailed options for fitting the applied gate.

modules.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_builder
documentation: Build specific tensor networks, including states and operators.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_builder.delta_array
documentation:
Get the delta symbol AKA COPY-tensor as an ndarray.

Parameters
----------
shape : tuple[int]
    The shape of the array, not all dimensions have to match.
dtype : str, optional
    The data type of the array.

Returns
-------
numpy.ndarray

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_builder.rand_tensor
documentation:
Generate a random tensor with specified shape and inds.

Parameters
----------
shape : sequence of int
    Size of each dimension.
inds : sequence of str
    Names of each dimension.
tags : sequence of str
    Labels to tag this tensor with.
dtype : {'float64', 'complex128', 'float32', 'complex64'}, optional
    The underlying data type.
dist : {'normal', 'uniform', 'rademacher', 'exp'}, optional
    Type of random number to generate, defaults to 'normal'.
scale : float, optional
    A multiplicative factor to scale the random numbers by.
loc : float, optional
    An additive offset to add to the random numbers.
left_inds : sequence of str, optional
    Which, if any, indices to group as 'left' indices of an effective
    matrix. This can be useful, for example, when automatically applying
    unitary constraints to impose a certain flow on a tensor network but at
    the atomistic (Tensor) level.

Returns
-------
Tensor

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_builder.rand_phased
documentation:
Generate a random tensor with specified shape and inds, and randomly
'phased' (distributed on the unit circle) data, such that
`T.H @ T == T.norm()**2 == T.size`.

# Parameters

shape

: Size of each dimension.

inds

: Names of each dimension.

tags

: Labels to tag this tensor with.

dtype

: The underlying data type - can only be complex.

# Returns

Tensor

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_builder.rand_symmetric_array
documentation:
Get a random symmetric array, i.e. one that is invariant under
permutation of its indices. It has `(ndim + d - 1) choose (d - 1)` unique
elements.

Parameters
----------
d : int
    The size of each dimension.
ndim : int
    The number of dimensions.
dist : {'normal', 'uniform', 'rademacher', 'exp'}, optional
    Type of random number to generate, defaults to 'normal'.
loc : float, optional
    An additive offset to add to the random numbers.
scale : float, optional
    A multiplicative factor to scale the random numbers by.
seed : int, optional
    A random seed.
dtype : {'float64', 'complex128', 'float32', 'complex64'}, optional
    The underlying data type.
fill_fn : callable, optional
    A function that takes a shape (here always `()`) and returns an array,
    rather that using the random number generator.

Returns

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_builder.TN_from_edges_and_fill_fn
documentation:
Create a tensor network from a sequence of edges defining a graph,
and a 'fill' function that maps shapes to data.

# Parameters

fill_fn

: A function with signature `fill_fn(shape) -> array`, used to fill
  each tensor.

edges

: The graph edges, as a sequence of pairs of hashable objects, for
  example integers, representing the nodes. You can redundantly specify
  `(u, v)` and `(v, u)` and only one edge will be added.

D

: The bond dimension connecting tensors.

phys_dim

: If not `None`, give each tensor a 'physical', free index of this size
  at each node.

site_tag_id

: String with formatter to tag sites.

site_ind_id

: String with formatter to tag indices (if `phys_dim` specified). If a
  single str is supplied, the tensor network will have a single index at
  each site, representing a vector. If a pair of strings is supplied, the
  tensor network will have two indices at each site, representing an
  operator with upper and lower indices.

# Returns

TensorNetworkGen, TensorNetworkGenVector or TensorNetworkGenOperator

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_builder.TN2D_corner_double_line
documentation:
Build a 2D 'corner double line' (CDL) tensor network. Each plaquette
contributes a matrix (by default the identity) at each corner, connected in
a loop. The corners for each site are then grouped and optionally
contracted. Such a tensor network has strong local correlations. See
<https://arxiv.org/abs/1412.0732>. If the sites are not contracted, the
resulting network is a product of loops that can be easily and exactly
contracted.

Note that if identity matrices are used, the contracted value of the tensor
network is `line_dim**num_plaquettes`.

# Parameters

Lx

: Length of side x.

Ly

: Length of side y.

line_dim

: The dimension of the matrices at each corner. If `contract` is True,
  then the resulting bonds with have dimension `line_dim**tiling`.

tiling

: How to tile the plaquettes. If `1`, the plaquettes are tiled in a
  checkerboard pattern resulting in a single line per edge. If `2`, the
  plaquettes are tiled in a dense pattern resulting in two lines per
  edge.

fill_missing_edges

: Whether to fill in the missing edges around the border with open
  strings, ensuring every bond exists and has the same dimension.

site_tag_id

: String specifier for naming convention of site tags.

x_tag_id

: String specifier for naming convention of row tags.

y_tag_id

: String specifier for naming convention of column tags.

kwargs

: Additional keyword arguments are passed to {func}`TN_from_strings`.

# Returns

TensorNetwork2D

# See Also

TN_from_strings

keep the matrix real, but it must be paired with the opposite in a tensor

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_builder.HTN2D_classical_ising_partition_function
documentation:
Hyper tensor network representation of the 2D classical ising model
partition function. The indices will be shared by 4 or 5 tensors depending
on whether `h` is non-zero. As opposed to the 'normal' tensor network,
here each classical spin is still a single index, which is easier to
contract exactly.

# Parameters

Lx

: Length of side x.

Ly

: Length of side y.

beta

: The inverse temperature.

h

: The magnetic field strength.

j

: The interaction strength, positive being *ferromagnetic*. If a dict
  should contain entries for each edge, keyed by the edge. If a callable
  should have the signature `j(node_a, node_b)` and return a float.

cyclic

: Whether to use periodic boundary conditions. X and Y can be specified
  separately using a tuple.

ind_id

: How to label the indices i.e. `ind_id.format(i, j)`, each of which
  corresponds to a single classical spin.

site_tag_id

: How to label the site tags, note that in the hyper tensor network
  representation each tensor will have two site tags for the two sites it
  connects.

# Returns

TensorNetwork

# See Also

TN2D_classical_ising_partition_function

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_builder.HTN3D_classical_ising_partition_function
documentation:
Hyper tensor network representation of the 3D classical ising model
partition function. The indices will be shared by 6 or 7 tensors depending
on whether `h` is non-zero. As opposed to the 'normal' tensor network,
here each classical spin is still a single index, which is easier to
contract exactly.

# Parameters

Lx

: Length of side x.

Ly

: Length of side y.

Lz

: Length of side z.

beta

: The inverse temperature.

j

: The interaction strength, positive being *ferromagnetic*. If a dict
  should contain entries for each edge, keyed by the edge. If a callable
  should have the signature `j(node_a, node_b)` and return a float.

h

: The magnetic field strength.

cyclic

: Whether to use periodic boundary conditions. X, Y and Z can be
  specified separately using a tuple.

ind_id

: How to label the indices i.e. `ind_id.format(i, j, k)`, each of which
  corresponds to a single classical spin.

site_tag_id

: How to label the site tags, note that in the hyper tensor network
  representation each tensor will have two site tags for the two sites it
  connects.

# Returns

TensorNetwork

# See Also

TN3D_classical_ising_partition_function

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_builder.HTN_classical_partition_function_from_edges
documentation:
Build a hyper tensor network representation of a classical ising model
partition function by specifying graph edges. There will be a single
tensor *per interaction* rather than per site, as well as a single tensor
for each site, if `h != 0.0`.

# Parameters

edges

: The graph edges, as a sequence of pairs of hashable objects, for
  example integers, representing the nodes. You can redundantly specify
  `(u, v)` and `(v, u)` and only one edge will be added.

beta

: The inverse temperature.

j

: The interaction strength, positive being *ferromagnetic*. If a dict
  should contain entries for each edge, keyed by the edge. If a callable
  should have the signature `j(node_a, node_b)` and return a float.

h

: The magnetic field strength. If a callable should have the
  signature `h(node)` and return a float.

site_ind_id

: A string formatter for naming tensor indices like
  `site_ind_id.format(node)`.

site_tag_id

: A string formatter for naming tensor tags like
  `site_tag_id.format(node)`.

bond_tag_id

: A string formatter for naming tensor tags like
  `bond_tag_id.format(node_a, node_b)`.

# Returns

TensorNetwork

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_builder.TN_classical_partition_function_from_edges
documentation:
Build a regular tensor network representation of a classical ising model
partition function by specifying graph edges. There will be a single
tensor per site.

# Parameters

edges

: The graph edges, as a sequence of pairs of hashable objects, for
  example integers, representing the nodes. You can redundantly specify
  `(u, v)` and `(v, u)` and only one edge will be added.

beta

: The inverse temperature.

j

: The interaction strength, positive being *ferromagnetic*. If a dict
  should contain entries for each edge, keyed by the edge. If a callable
  should have the signature `j(node_a, node_b)` and return a float.

h

: The magnetic field strength. If a callable should have the
  signature `h(node)` and return a float.

site_tag_id

: A string formatter for naming tensor tags like
  `site_ind_id.format(node)`.

bond_ind_id

: A string formatter for naming the indices bewteen tensors like
  `bond_ind_id.format(node_a, node_b)`.

# Returns

TensorNetwork

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_builder.make_couplings_matrix_symmetric
documentation:
Take a coupling matrix or dict of pairwise strengths in either upper or
lower triangular form and return a symmetric matrix.

# Parameters

J

: The couplings, as a square matrix or explicit dict. If a matrix, the
  upper or lower triangle (corresponding to `UPLO`) should the pairwise
  interaction strengths. If a dict, then each non-zero interaction can be
  specified explicitly.

UPLO

: Whether to read the couplings in from `J` in lower ('L': `i >= j`)
  or upper ('U': `i <= j`) form. If `J` is a dict then this only
  matters if both are present and in that case acts as a preference.
  If `"auto"` then either the lower or upper, or symmetric distances
  can be supplied, but an error is raised if both are.

# Returns

J

: The couplings as a symmetric matrix.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_builder.TN2D_embedded_classical_ising_partition_function
documentation:
Construct a (triangular) '2D' tensor network representation of the
classical ising model partition function with all-to-all interactions, by
embedding it in a 2D lattice. For `N` spins this will have `N(N-1)/2`
tensors, present at sites `(i, j)` where `i > j`. If `outputs` is supplied,
then dangling indices will be added for each output, to give a 'standard'
TN representation of the unnormalized marginal distribution over those
outputs. Since each spin is effectively "delocalized" into a COPY-tensor
MPS spanning the lattice, there is a choice of where to put the outputs,
specified by `sites_location`.

`sites_location="side"`:

```
...   .  .  .  ╭──
               │
j=2   .  .  ╭──O──...
            │  │
j=1   .  ╭──O──O──...
         │  │  │
j=0   ╭──O──O──O──...
      │  │  │  │
      s0 s1 s2 s3  <- site indices

    i=0, 1, 2, 3, ...
```

`sites_location="diag"`:

```
...   .  .  s3 ╭──
              ╲│
j=2   .  s2 ╭──O──...
           ╲│  │
j=1   s1 ╭──O──O──...
        ╲│  │  │
j=0  s0──O──O──O──...

    i=0, 1, 2, 3, ...
```

# Parameters

Jij

: The couplings, as a square matrix or explicit dict. If a matrix, the
  upper or lower triangle (corresponding to `UPLO`) should the pairwise
  interaction strengths. If a dict, then each non-zero interaction can be
  specified explicitly.

beta

: The inverse temperature.

outputs

: Which sites to generate output indices (i.e. dangling legs) for.
  The index is named according to `ind_id`. The location of the output
  depends on `sites_location`.

ordering

: If supplied, the ordering of the embedded spins into the lattice.

sites_location

: Whether to place the output indices on the side or diagonally. If
  `"side"` then the output indices will be placed along the 'y-min'
  border, i.e. `[(j, 0) for j in range(1, N)]`, with two on the first
  tensor. If `"diag"` then the output indices will be placed along the
  central diagonal fold of the lattice, i.e.
  `[(j + 1, j) for j in range(1, N)]`, with two on the first tensor.

UPLO

: Whether to read the couplings in from `J` in lower ('L': `i >= j`)
  or upper ('U': `i <= j`) form. If `J` is a dict then this only
  matters if both are present and in that case acts as a preference.
  If `"auto"` then either the lower or upper, or symmetric distances
  can be supplied, but an error is raised if both are.

contract_sites

: Whether to contract the sites of the TN, which after initial
  construction will have one horizontol spin-rail, one vertical spin-rail
  and one interaction tensor per site.

site_tag_id

: String formatter specifying how to label each site.

x_tag_id

: String formatter specifying how to label each x-plane.

y_tag_id

: String formatter specifying how to label each y-plane.

ind_id

: How to label the indices i.e. `ind_id.format(i)`, each of
  which corresponds to a single classical spin in `outputs`.

# Returns

TensorNetwork2D

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_builder.TN_dimer_covering_from_edges
documentation:
Make a tensor network from sequence of graph edges that counts the
number of ways to cover the graph exactly with dimers. See
<https://arxiv.org/abs/1805.10598> for the construction.

# Parameters

edges

: The edges, each item should be a pair of hashable objects describing
  nodes linked.

cover_count

: The exact number of times each node must be 'covered'. For example
  1 for a standard dimer covering or 2 for 'ice rules'.

site_tag_id

: A string formatter for naming tensor tags like
  `site_ind_id.format(node)`.

bond_ind_id

: A string formatter for naming the indices bewteen tensors like
  `bond_ind_id.format(node_a, node_b)`.

# Returns

TensorNetwork

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_builder.HTN_from_clauses
documentation:
Given a list of clauses, create a hyper tensor network, with a single
hyper index for each variable, and single tensor or tensor decomposition
for each clause. If weights are given, there will also be a single tensor
for each non-trivially weighted variable.

# Parameters

clauses

: The clauses as a sequence of tuples of integers. Each integer
  represents a variable, and the sign indicates whether it is negated.
  The variables thus must be non-zero integers.

weights

: The weights for each variable. Each key should be a signed variable
  integer, such that relative weights for a variable `v` are
  `(weights[-v], weights[v])`. If only one is given of this pair, the
  other is assumed to sum to 1. If a variable is not supplied, or
  `weights=None`, then both weights are assumed to be 1 and no tensor
  is created for the variable.

mode

: How to represent the clauses:

  > - 'parafac' - `N` rank-2 tensors connected by a single hyper index.
  >   You could further call {meth}`hyperinds_resolve` for more options
  >   to convert the hyper index into a (decomposed) COPY-tensor.
  > - 'mps' - `N` rank-3 tensors connected along a 1D line.
  > - 'dense' - contract the hyper index.
  > - int - use the 'parafac' mode, but only if the length of a clause
  >   is larger than this threshold.

  Note that variables are always represented by a single (hyper) index,
  which is like an implicit PARAFAC decomposition.

dtype

: The data type of the tensors.

clause_tag_id

: The tag to use for the clause tensors. The tag will be formatted with
  the clause index.

var_ind_id

: The index to use for the variable tensors. The index will be formatted
  with the variable index.

weight_tag_id

: The tag to use for the weight tensors. The tag will be formatted with
  the variable index.

# Returns

htn : TensorNetwork

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_builder.cnf_file_parse
documentation:
Parse a DIMACS style 'cnf' file into a list of clauses, and possibly a
dictionary of weights. The weights, if present, can be specified either as:

> - (CACHET format): a line per weight like `w {signed_var} {weight}`,
>   where `signed_var` is an integer whose sign specifies the sign of
>   the weight being set.
> - (MC2021 competition format): the same as above, but with each line
>   specified as `c p weight {signed_var} {weight}`.
> - (MINIC2D format): a single line of the form
>   ``` c weights {wp_1} {wm_1} {wp_2} {wm_2}... ``,   where ``wp_i ``` and
>   `wn_i` are the positive and negative weights for variable `i`.
>   Weights specified this way are overriden by the previous two formats.

# Parameters

fname

: Path to a '.cnf' or '.wcnf' file.

# Returns

instance : dict

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_builder.HTN_from_cnf
documentation:
Create a hyper tensor network from a '.cnf' or '.wcnf' file - i.e. a
model counting or weighted model counting instance specification.

# Parameters

fname

: Path to a '.cnf' or '.wcnf' file.

mode

: How to represent the clauses:

  > - 'parafac' - `N` rank-2 tensors connected by a single hyper index.
  >   You could further call {meth}`hyperinds_resolve` for more options
  >   to convert the hyper index into a (decomposed) COPY-tensor.
  > - 'mps' - `N` rank-3 tensors connected along a 1D line.
  > - 'dense' - contract the hyper index.
  > - int - use the 'parafac' mode, but only if the length of a clause
  >   is larger than this threshold.

dtype

: Data type of the tensors.

clause_tag_id

: Format string for clause tags.

var_ind_id

: Format string for variable indices.

weight_tag_id

: Format string for weight tags.

kwargs

: Additional keyword arguments passed to {func}`HTN_from_clauses`.

# Returns

htn : TensorNetwork

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_builder.random_ksat_instance
documentation:
Create a random k-SAT instance.

Parameters
----------
k : int
    Number of variables per clause.
num_variables : int
    Number of variables in the instance.
num_clauses : int, optional
    Number of clauses in the instance. If not specified, will be
    determined from `alpha`.
alpha : float, optional
    If `num_clauses` is not directly specified then the average number of
    clauses *per variable*. Taken as a Poisson parameter. Either this or
    `num_clauses` must be specified.
seed : int, optional
    Random seed.
allow_repeat_variables : bool, optional
    Whether to allow the same variable to appear multiple times in a
    single clause.

Returns
-------
instance : dict
    Dictionary with keys 'num_variables', 'num_clauses', 'clauses'. The
    'clauses' key contains a list of tuples, each tuple representing a
    clause. Within each tuple, each element is an integer representing a
    variable, with the sign of the integer representing the sign of the
    variable in the clause.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_builder.HTN_random_ksat
documentation:
Create a random k-SAT instance encoded as a hyper tensor network.

# Parameters

k

: Number of variables per clause.

num_variables

: Number of variables in the instance.

num_clauses

: Number of clauses in the instance. If not specified, will be
  determined from `alpha`.

alpha

: If `num_clauses` is not directly specified then the average number of
  clauses *per variable*. Taken as a Poisson parameter. Either this or
  `num_clauses` must be specified.

seed

: Random seed.

allow_repeat_variables

: Whether to allow the same variable to appear multiple times in a
  single clause.

mode

: How to represent the clauses:

  > - 'parafac' - `N` rank-2 tensors connected by a single hyper index.
  >   You could further call {meth}`hyperinds_resolve` for more options
  >   to convert the hyper index into a (decomposed) COPY-tensor.
  > - 'mps' - `N` rank-3 tensors connected along a 1D line.
  > - 'dense' - contract the hyper index.
  > - int - use the 'parafac' mode, but only if the length of a clause
  >   is larger than this threshold.

  Note that variables are always represented by a single (hyper) index,
  which is like an implicit PARAFAC decomposition.

dtype

: Data type of the tensors.

clause_tag_id

: Format string for clause tags. Should contain a single `{}` which
  will be replaced by the clause number.

variable_ind_id

: Format string for variable indices. Should contain a single `{}` which
  will be replaced by the variable number.

# Returns

TensorNetwork

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_builder.TN_matching
documentation:
Create a tensor network with the same outer indices as `tn` but
with a single tensor per site with bond dimension `max_bond` between
each connected site. Generally to be used as an initial guess for fitting.

# Parameters

tn

: The tensor network to match, it can have arbitrary local structure and
  output indices, as long as `site_tags` effectively partitions it.

max_bond

: The bond dimension to use between each site.

site_tags

: The tags to use to select the tensors from `tn`. If not given, uses
  `tn.site_tags`. The tensor network built will have one tensor per
  site, in the order given by `site_tags`.

dtype

: The data type to use for the new tensors, if not given uses the same as
  the original tensors.

randn_opts

: Supplied to {func}`~quimb.gen.rand.randn`.

# Returns

TensorNetwork

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_builder.MPS_rand_state

scale

trans_invar

mps_opts

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_builder.spin_ham_mpo_tensor
documentation:
Generate tensor(s) for a spin hamiltonian MPO.

# Parameters

one_site_terms

: The terms that act on a single site, each `operator` can be a string
  suitable to be sent to {func}`spin_operator` or an actual 2d-array.

two_site_terms

: The terms that act on two neighbouring sites, each `operator` can be
  a string suitable to be sent to {func}`spin_operator` or an actual
  2d-array.

S

: What size spin to use, defaults to spin-1/2.

left_two_site_terms

: If the interaction to the left of this site has different spin terms
  then the equivalent list of terms for that site.

which

: If `None`, generate the middle tensor, if 'L' a left-end tensor, if
  'R' a right-end tensor and if 'A' all three.

cyclic

: Whether to use periodic boundary conditions - default False.

# Returns

numpy.ndarray[, numpy.ndarray, numpy.ndarray]

: The middle, left, right or all three MPO tensors.

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_builder.MPO_ham_ising
documentation:
Ising Hamiltonian in MPO form.

$$
H_\mathrm{Ising} =
J \sum_{i} S^Z_i S^Z_{i + 1} -
B_x \sum_{i} S^X_i
$$

Note the default convention of antiferromagnetic interactions and spin
operators not Pauli matrices.

# Parameters

L

: The number of sites.

j

: The ZZ interaction strength. Positive is antiferromagnetic.

bx

: The X-magnetic field strength.

S

: The underlying spin of the system, defaults to 1/2.

cyclic

: Generate a MPO with periodic boundary conditions or not, default is
  open boundary conditions.

mpo_opts or local_ham_1d_opts

: Supplied to {class}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_1d.MatrixProductOperator`.

# Returns

MatrixProductOperator

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_builder.ham_1d_ising
documentation:
Ising Hamiltonian in
{class}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_1d_tebd.LocalHam1D` form.

$$
H_\mathrm{Ising} =
J \sum_{i} S^Z_i S^Z_{i + 1} -
B_x \sum_{i} S^X_i
$$

Note the default convention of antiferromagnetic interactions and spin
operators not Pauli matrices.

# Parameters

L

: The number of sites.

j

: The ZZ interaction strength. Positive is antiferromagnetic.

bx

: The X-magnetic field strength.

S

: The underlying spin of the system, defaults to 1/2.

cyclic

: Generate a hamiltonian with periodic boundary conditions or not,
  default is open boundary conditions.

mpo_opts or local_ham_1d_opts

: Supplied to {class}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_1d.LocalHam1D`.

# Returns

LocalHam1D

functions.name: quimb.tensor.tensor_builder.MPO_ham_XY
documentation:
XY-Hamiltonian in MPO form.

$$
H_\mathrm{XY} =
\sum_{i} (
    J_X S^X_i S^X_{i + 1} +
    J_Y S^Y_i S^Y_{i + 1}
    )
- B_x \sum_{i} S^Z_i
$$

Note the default convention of antiferromagnetic interactions and spin
operators not Pauli matrices.

# Parameters

L

: The number of sites.

j

: The XX and YY interaction strength. Positive is antiferromagnetic.

bz

: The Z-magnetic field strength.

S

: The underlying spin of the system, defaults to 1/2.

cyclic

: Generate a MPO with periodic boundary conditions or not, default is
  open boundary conditions.

mpo_opts or local_ham_1d_opts

: Supplied to {class}`~quimb.tensor.tensor_1d.MatrixProductOperator`.

MatrixProductOperator

$$

# Parameters

j

H_\mathrm{Heis} =

    J_Z S^Z_i S^Z_{i + 1}
    )
- B_Z \sum_{i} S^Z_i
$$

Note the default convention of antiferromagnetic interactions and spin

# Parameters

L

j

: The XX, YY and ZZ interaction strength. Positive is antiferromagnetic.

bz
